cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a null pointer dereference vulnerability in the gf_isom_get_payt_count function in hint_track.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service.

undesired_operation.description: hmhd is dereferenced via hmhd->type before the NULL check on hmhd, causing a null pointer dereference if trak->Media->information->InfoHeader is NULL
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (null pointer dereference)

initial_state.value: NULL (cast from trak->Media->information->InfoHeader which is NULL)

step_by_step_execution.description: hmhd is assigned from trak->Media->information->InfoHeader cast to GF_HintMediaHeaderBox*

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: hmhd is NULL, so dereferencing hmhd->type causes a null pointer dereference before the condition can be evaluated

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches the CVE: a null pointer dereference of hmhd->type before checking if hmhd is NULL, fixed by adding !hmhd check before the dereference.

actual_issue_summary: In GetHintFormat(), hmhd (derived from InfoHeader) is dereferenced via hmhd->type before being checked for NULL. The fix adds a !hmhd check before accessing hmhd->type.

cve_desc: Flatpak is a Linux application sandboxing and distribution framework. Prior to versions 1.12.3 and 1.10.6, Flatpak doesn't properly validate that the permissions displayed to the user for an app at install time match the actual permissions granted to the app at runtime, in the case that there's a null byte in the metadata file of an app. Therefore apps can grant themselves permissions without the consent of the user. Flatpak shows permissions to the user during install by reading them from the "xa.metadata" key in the commit metadata. This cannot contain a null terminator, because it is an untrusted GVariant. Flatpak compares these permissions to the *actual* metadata, from the "metadata" file to ensure it wasn't lied to. However, the actual metadata contents are loaded in several places where they are read as simple C-style strings. That means that, if the metadata file includes a null terminator, only the content of the file from *before* the terminator gets compared to xa.metadata. Thus, any permissions that appear in the metadata file after a null terminator are applied at runtime but not shown to the user. So maliciously crafted apps can give themselves hidden permissions. Users who have Flatpaks installed from untrusted sources are at risk in case the Flatpak has a maliciously crafted metadata file, either initially or in an update. This issue is patched in versions 1.12.3 and 1.10.6. As a workaround, users can manually check the permissions of installed apps by checking the metadata file or the xa.metadata key on the commit metadata.

undesired_operation.description: When no extra_gpg_data is provided by the caller, the function uses GPG keys included within the bundle itself (included_gpg_data) to configure the remote for signature verification. This allows a malicious bundle to supply its own signing keys, enabling it to pass GPG verification checks during the subsequent pull operation.
code_snippets: if (!flatpak_dir_modify_remote (self, remote, new_config, gpg_data, cancellable, error))
remote = flatpak_dir_create_origin_remote (self, origin, id, basename, flatpak_decomposed_get_ref (ref), gpg_data, collection_id, &created_remote, cancellable, error);
undesired_operation.impact: An attacker who crafts a malicious Flatpak bundle can embed their own GPG keys inside it. When a user installs this bundle without providing separate trusted GPG data (extra_gpg_data is NULL), the attacker's keys are imported into the remote configuration and used to verify the bundle's own signature, bypassing GPG signature verification entirely.

initial_state.value: NULL (caller does not provide trusted GPG data)

step_by_step_execution.description: flatpak_bundle_load extracts included_gpg_data from the bundle file
gpg_data is set to included_gpg_data since extra_gpg_data is NULL
gpg_data is used to configure the remote, importing attacker's keys for verification

out_state.value: attacker-controlled GPG keys (now imported into remote config)

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential GPG key trust issue unrelated to CVE-2021-43860. The actual CVE is about bundles without metadata bypassing permission display to users during install. The fix adds a check that fp_metadata is not NULL when metadata output is requested, ensuring permissions are properly presented. The GPG key handling code was not changed by the fix and is a separate design choice in Flatpak's bundle handling.

actual_issue_summary: When installing a Flatpak bundle that lacks metadata in its header, the function would proceed without error even when the caller needed metadata (e.g., to display permissions to the user). This meant permissions would not be properly presented during the transaction, allowing apps to be installed without the user seeing or consenting to the permissions granted. The fix adds a check that fp_metadata is not NULL when out_metadata is requested, failing with an error if the bundle has no metadata.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The `simplifyBroadcast` function in the MLIR-TFRT infrastructure in TensorFlow is vulnerable to a segfault (hence, denial of service), if called with scalar shapes. If all shapes are scalar, then `maxRank` is 0, so we build an empty `SmallVector`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. This is the only affected version.

undesired_operation.description: When maxRank is 0 (e.g., all input shapes have rank 0, or shapes is empty), shape_and_rank_for_dim is an empty SmallVector. llvm::is_splat is called on an empty range (which has undefined behavior / asserts non-empty), and then shape_and_rank_for_dim[0] performs an out-of-bounds access on the empty vector.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or assertion failure. Potentially exploitable out-of-bounds read if assertions are disabled.

initial_state.value: input ValueRange, all shapes are scalar (rank 0)

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop over shapes computes maxRank as 0 because all shapes are scalar
shape_and_rank_for_dim is constructed as empty SmallVector of size maxRank=0
Inner loops over shapes_found dimensions do nothing since all shapes have rank 0

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: is_splat on an empty range triggers undefined behavior or assertion failure, causing a segfault

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue of CVE-2022-23593: when all shapes are scalar, maxRank is 0, resulting in empty vectors. The code then crashes when accessing these empty vectors (via is_splat or the final FromElementsOp call). The fix adds an early return when maxRank == 0, which is exactly what the finding describes as missing. While the CVE description specifically mentions the segfault from FromElementsOp with an empty vector, the finding identifies a closely related crash path in the same function due to the same root cause (maxRank == 0 with no guard).

actual_issue_summary: When simplifyBroadcast is called with all scalar shapes, maxRank is 0, leading to empty SmallVectors. The code proceeds to call tensor::FromElementsOp with an empty elements vector and no explicit type, causing a segfault. The fix adds an early return when maxRank == 0 that properly constructs the FromElementsOp with an explicit type.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `GetInitOp` is vulnerable to a crash caused by dereferencing a null pointer. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The result of .find(kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey) on the outputs map is dereferenced without checking if it equals .end(). If the signature_def contains kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey but its outputs map does not, this dereferences an end() iterator, causing undefined behavior (out-of-bounds read/crash).
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential information disclosure from reading invalid memory. An attacker who can supply a crafted SavedModel (MetaGraphDef) where the signature_def contains the init op signature key but its outputs map does not contain that same key can trigger this.

state.value: valid iterator pointing to entry in sig_def_map (not end())
end() iterator (key not found in outputs map)

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled map that contains kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey as a key

step_by_step_execution.description: Find kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey in signature_def map

out_state.value: valid iterator pointing to the entry for kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The key exists in signature_def, so the iterator is valid and not end()

step_by_step_execution.description: Call .find(kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey) on init_op_sig_it->second.outputs(). The outputs map does not contain this key, so find returns end(). The code immediately dereferences this end() iterator via ->second.name().

in_state.value: map that does NOT contain kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey

out_state.value: end() iterator, dereferenced causing undefined behavior / null pointer dereference / crash

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-23577: the null/invalid pointer dereference in GetInitOp caused by not checking whether the inner find() on the outputs map returns end() before dereferencing. The fix adds exactly this check.

actual_issue_summary: In GetInitOp, the code checks that kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey exists in the outer signature_def map, but then calls .find(kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey) on the inner outputs map and dereferences the result without checking if the key was found. If the outputs map doesn't contain that key, this dereferences an end() iterator (null pointer dereference), causing a crash.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library. In version 2.11.1 and prior, if incoming RTCP XR message contain block, the data field is not checked against the received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP XR. A malicious actor can send a RTCP XR message with an invalid packet size.

undesired_operation.description: The code casts rb_hdr to large struct types (e.g., pjmedia_rtcp_xr_rb_voip_mtc, pjmedia_rtcp_xr_rb_stats) and later reads fields at large offsets without verifying that the report block's length (rb_len) is sufficient for the struct type. An attacker can craft a packet where rb_hdr is near the end of the validated packet region, set bt to BT_VOIP_METRICS, and set rb_len to a small value like 1. The while loop only checks that rb_hdr itself is within bounds, not that the entire struct is. Later, accessing fields like rb_voip_mtc->jb_abs_max reads far beyond the packet buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from a network-controlled packet. An attacker can read memory beyond the packet buffer, potentially causing a crash (denial of service) or information disclosure if the read data is reflected back.

state.value: points near end of packet buffer, within bounds of pkt_len but not enough space for full struct
cast from rb_hdr, struct extends beyond packet buffer

initial_state.value: points to rtcp_xr->buf (start of report blocks)

step_by_step_execution.description: pkt_len is read from the RTCP XR common header length field

out_state.value: attacker-controlled value, validated such that (pkt_len+1) <= size/4

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: rb_hdr starts at rtcp_xr->buf which is within the packet, and pkt_len can be set large enough for the header to be within bounds

relevant_state.value: points to a valid position within the packet but near the end

step_by_step_execution.description: rb_len is read from rb_hdr->length; attacker sets it to a small value like 1

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: rb_len is 1, which is non-zero
Attacker sets the block type to BT_VOIP_METRICS in the crafted packet

step_by_step_execution.description: rb_voip_mtc is set to rb_hdr without checking that the full struct fits within the packet

out_state.value: points to rb_hdr, but sizeof(*rb_voip_mtc) extends beyond packet buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: Fields of rb_voip_mtc are read, including jb_abs_max at a large offset, causing out-of-bounds read

in_state.value: points near end of packet, struct extends beyond allocated buffer

out_state.value: read from memory beyond packet buffer (OOB read)

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-43845: the RTCP XR block data fields are not checked against received packet size, leading to out-of-bounds read. The fix adds bounds checks for each block type before casting, which is precisely what this finding identifies.

actual_issue_summary: In pjmedia_rtcp_xr_rx_rtcp_xr(), when parsing RTCP XR report blocks, the code casts rb_hdr to specific block struct types without verifying that the full struct size fits within the received packet. This allows an attacker to send a crafted RTCP XR packet with block headers that claim a certain type but the packet is too small to contain the full struct, resulting in out-of-bounds reads when accessing struct fields. The fix adds bounds checks comparing rb_hdr + sizeof(struct) against pkt + size before each cast.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `AssignOp` can result in copying uninitialized data to a new tensor. This later results in undefined behavior. The implementation has a check that the left hand side of the assignment is initialized (to minimize number of allocations), but does not check that the right hand side is also initialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: When use_exclusive_lock_ is false and the else branch is taken (num_elements differ), a newly allocated but uninitialized copy_tensor is installed as the ref input while the lock is held, but the actual Copy of data into it happens after the lock is released. Between lock release and Copy, any concurrent reader of the variable will observe uninitialized heap memory.
code_snippets:
Tensor old_unlocked_lhs = context->mutable_input(0, /* lock_held */ false);
Copy(context, &old_unlocked_lhs, rhs);
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure — a concurrent reader of the variable observes uninitialized heap memory, potentially leaking sensitive data from prior allocations.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is specifically about the right-hand side (rhs) tensor not being checked for initialization before being used in Copy operations. The fix adds `rhs.IsInitialized()` check at the top of the function. The finding describes a different issue — a race condition where copy_tensor (a newly allocated tensor) could be observed by concurrent readers before the Copy fills it. While both involve uninitialized data (CWE-908), the finding identifies a different root cause and code path than what the CVE and its fix address. The CVE's concern is that an attacker can pass an uninitialized rhs tensor, not a race condition on copy_tensor.

actual_issue_summary: The AssignOp implementation checks that the left-hand side (lhs) tensor is initialized but never checks that the right-hand side (rhs) tensor is initialized. If rhs is uninitialized, its data gets copied to the output tensor, resulting in use of uninitialized memory and undefined behavior. The fix adds an `rhs.IsInitialized()` check at the beginning of the Compute method.

cve_desc: uWebSockets 18.11.0 and 18.12.0 has a stack-based buffer overflow in uWS::TopicTree::trimTree (called from uWS::TopicTree::unsubscribeAll). NOTE: the vendor's position is that this is "a minor issue or not even an issue at all" because the developer of an application (that uses uWebSockets) should not be allowing the large number of triggered topics to accumulate

undesired_operation.description: triggeredTopics array (fixed size 64) is written out of bounds because the bounds check (numTriggeredTopics == 64) only occurs once at function entry, but multiple increments of numTriggeredTopics happen within the loop body and after the loop without further bounds checks.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds write past the triggeredTopics array, potentially leading to memory corruption, crash, or arbitrary code execution.

state.value: can exceed 64 due to multiple increments after the single entry check

initial_state.value: any value 0..64 (checked only once at entry)

step_by_step_execution.description: At function entry, if numTriggeredTopics == 64, drain() is called resetting to 0. Otherwise no check.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Topic string contains '/' separators so the loop iterates multiple times
The topic tree has terminating wildcard children along the path
This wildcard child has not been triggered yet

step_by_step_execution.description: First increment: triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++] writes at index 63
Loop continues to next segment, another terminatingWildcardChild is found and not yet triggered

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: A different wildcard child, not yet triggered

step_by_step_execution.description: Second increment: triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++] writes at index 64, which is OUT OF BOUNDS

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the CVE-2020-36406 vulnerability: the drain() check at function entry is insufficient because multiple increments to numTriggeredTopics can occur within the loop, causing writes beyond the 64-element triggeredTopics array. The fix moves the bounds check to immediately before each increment, which is exactly what the commit does.

actual_issue_summary: The drain() call that prevents triggeredTopics array overflow was placed only at function entry, but multiple topics could be triggered within a single publish() call (via the loop and recursive calls). This allowed numTriggeredTopics to exceed 64, causing a stack-based buffer overflow when writing to the fixed-size triggeredTopics[64] array. The fix moves the overflow check to immediately before each array write.

cve_desc: Depending on the way the format strings in the card label are crafted it's possible to leak kernel stack memory. There is also the possibility for DoS due to the v4l2loopback kernel module crashing when providing the card label on request (reproduce e.g. with many %s modifiers in a row).

undesired_operation.description: snprintf is called with dev->card_label as the format string argument. If card_label contains format specifiers (e.g., %s, %x, %n), snprintf will interpret them, leading to information disclosure from stack memory or potentially arbitrary writes via %n.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure of stack/kernel memory contents via format specifiers like %x/%p in card_label, or potential arbitrary kernel memory write via %n, leading to privilege escalation or denial of service.

state.value: attacker-controlled string containing format specifiers like %s, %x, %n

initial_state.value: user-controlled card label string, potentially containing format specifiers

step_by_step_execution.description: labellen is computed as the minimum of sizeof(cap->card) and sizeof(dev->card_label)
snprintf is called with dev->card_label directly as the format string, allowing any format specifiers in card_label to be interpreted

in_state.value: attacker-controlled string with format specifiers e.g. '%x%x%x%s'

out_state.value: contains formatted output interpreting stack values via format specifiers

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-2652: dev->card_label is passed as the format string to snprintf instead of using "%s" as the format specifier. The fix changes `snprintf(cap->card, labellen, dev->card_label)` to `snprintf(cap->card, labellen, "%s", dev->card_label)`. The CWE-134 (format string vulnerability) matches as well.

actual_issue_summary: In vidioc_querycap, dev->card_label (user-controlled) is passed directly as the format string argument to snprintf. A crafted card_label with format specifiers like %s or %x can leak kernel stack memory or cause a kernel crash (DoS). The fix adds an explicit "%s" format specifier.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. Under certain scenarios, Grappler component of TensorFlow can trigger a null pointer dereference. There are 2 places where this can occur, for the same malicious alteration of a `SavedModel` file (fixing the first one would trigger the same dereference in the second place). First, during constant folding, the `GraphDef` might not have the required nodes for the binary operation. If a node is missing, the correposning `mul_*child` would be null, and the dereference in the subsequent line would be incorrect. We have a similar issue during `IsIdentityConsumingSwitch`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: graph.GetNode() returns nullptr when the node name is not found in the graph, and the result is dereferenced without a null check via IsSwitch(*input_node)
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (null pointer dereference). An attacker who can supply a graph where an Identity node's input references a non-existent node triggers the crash.

initial_state.value: An Identity or IdentityNSingleInput node with at least one input

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The node is an Identity node with inputs

step_by_step_execution.description: Parse tensor name from node's first input

out_state.value: parsed tensor id with non-existent node name

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The tensor id is not a control dependency

step_by_step_execution.description: graph.GetNode() looks up the node name and returns nullptr because the node does not exist
input_node is dereferenced via IsSwitch(*input_node), causing null pointer dereference

out_state.value: crash due to null pointer dereference

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches the CVE: a null pointer dereference in IsIdentityConsumingSwitch when graph.GetNode() returns nullptr, which is one of the two places described in CVE-2022-23589. The diff adds exactly the null check this finding identifies as missing.

actual_issue_summary: In TensorFlow's Grappler component, the IsIdentityConsumingSwitch function dereferences the return value of graph.GetNode() without checking for nullptr. If the referenced node doesn't exist in the graph (e.g., from a maliciously altered SavedModel), this causes a null pointer dereference crash. The fix adds a nullptr check before the dereference.

cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in Homebrew mruby prior to 3.2.

undesired_operation.description: When val=0, codegen calls in the else branch do not push values onto the compile-time stack (since val=0 means the value is not needed). However, len is still incremented. If a NODE_KW_REST_ARGS node is later encountered, pop_n(len*2) is called, decrementing the stack pointer by len*2 even though those values were never pushed. This causes a stack pointer underflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack pointer underflow corrupts the compile-time stack pointer (cursp()), which can lead to out-of-bounds memory writes when subsequent codegen operations use the negative/underflowed stack index, potentially enabling code execution or a crash.

initial_state.value: non-NULL, list of hash key-value pairs followed by a NODE_KW_REST_ARGS node

step_by_step_execution.description: First iteration: the else branch is taken for a normal key-value pair. codegen is called twice with val=0, so nothing is pushed onto the stack. len is incremented to 1.

out_state.value: N (unchanged, since val=0 means no push)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: On the next iteration, the tree node is a NODE_KW_REST_ARGS node
len is 1, so the condition is true. The fix would check 'val && len > 0' which would be false since val=0.

step_by_step_execution.description: pop_n(len*2) is called, popping 2 entries from the compile-time stack even though nothing was pushed (val=0). This underflows the stack pointer.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the root cause of CVE-2022-0481: when val=0, pop_n and pop operations execute without corresponding pushes, causing a stack pointer underflow. The fix adds 'val &&' guards to prevent these pops when val=0. While the CVE is classified as CWE-476 (NULL Pointer Dereference) — likely the observable crash symptom — the finding identifies the underlying cause (stack underflow leading to the crash), which is the same vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: In gen_hash(), when val=0 (value not needed), the code still performed pop_n() and pop() operations inside the NODE_KW_REST_ARGS branch, even though no values were pushed onto the compile-time stack. This caused a stack pointer underflow, ultimately leading to a NULL pointer dereference. The fix adds 'val &&' guards to both conditional checks to prevent popping when val=0.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When decoding a tensor from protobuf, a TensorFlow process can encounter cases where a `CHECK` assertion is invalidated based on user controlled arguments, if the tensors have an invalid `dtype` and 0 elements or an invalid shape. This allows attackers to cause denial of services in TensorFlow processes. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: If TensorShape::num_elements() overflows due to attacker-controlled large dimensions in the proto, N can wrap to a small positive value. Helper<T>::Decode or FromProtoField<T> then allocates a buffer sized for the small N, but the Tensor's shape_ records the original large dimensions. Subsequent element access using the shape dimensions leads to heap buffer overflow.
code_snippets: CASES_WITH_DEFAULT(proto.dtype(), p = Helper<T>::Decode(a, content, N), dtype_error = true, dtype_error = true);
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — attacker provides a crafted TensorProto with dimensions whose product overflows int64_t, causing a small buffer allocation but a large logical tensor shape. Subsequent reads/writes to the tensor go out of bounds, enabling arbitrary code execution or information disclosure.

state.value: small positive value due to integer overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: N is computed from shape.num_elements() which could overflow

out_state.value: small positive value due to overflow

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: N is small positive due to overflow, dtype is valid

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a hypothetical integer overflow in num_elements() leading to heap buffer overflow. The actual CVE (CVE-2022-23571) is about an invalid dtype value bypassing validation when N <= 0 (empty tensor or incomplete shape), causing a CHECK/DCHECK assertion failure in set_dtype(). The fix adds dtype validation in the else branch for the N <= 0 case. This finding describes an entirely different issue.

undesired_operation.description: When num_elements() overflows to 0 or a negative value, the condition 'N > 0' is false, so no buffer is allocated (p remains nullptr). However, the function still sets shape_ to the attacker-controlled shape and buf_ to nullptr, creating a Tensor with large dimensions but a null data buffer. Any subsequent element access dereferences nullptr.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via null pointer dereference — attacker provides dimensions whose product overflows to zero or negative, resulting in a tensor with no backing buffer. Accessing elements crashes the program.

step_by_step_execution.description: N computed from overflowing num_elements()

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: N is 0 or negative

step_by_step_execution.description: shape_ and buf_ are set without validation

judgments.reasoning: This finding focuses on integer overflow causing N <= 0, leading to null pointer dereference. The actual CVE is about an invalid dtype (not DT_INVALID but an out-of-range enum value) reaching set_dtype() when N <= 0, triggering a CHECK assertion failure. While this finding correctly identifies that the N <= 0 path lacks validation, it misidentifies the root cause (integer overflow rather than invalid dtype) and the consequence (null deref rather than CHECK assertion failure). The fix validates dtype in the else branch, not shape dimensions.

actual_issue_summary: When a TensorProto has an invalid dtype (not DT_INVALID but an out-of-range/unrecognized enum value) and either 0 elements or an incomplete shape (N <= 0), the code skips the CASES_WITH_DEFAULT dtype validation (which only runs when N > 0) and proceeds to call set_dtype() with the invalid dtype value, triggering a CHECK/DCHECK assertion failure and crashing the process. The fix adds dtype validation in the else branch (when N <= 0) to reject invalid dtype values before reaching set_dtype().

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `StringNGrams` can be used to trigger a denial of service attack by causing an out of memory condition after an integer overflow. We are missing a validation on `pad_witdh` and that result in computing a negative value for `ngram_width` which is later used to allocate parts of the output. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: When splits tensor has exactly 2^31 elements, the cast to 'int splits_vec_size' overflows to INT_MIN (-2147483648). The validation block 'if (splits_vec_size > 0)' is skipped entirely since INT_MIN < 0.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from data array. Attacker can craft split values pointing to arbitrary memory locations, potentially causing information disclosure or crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: splits_vec.size() returns 2^31, cast to int overflows to INT_MIN

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: INT_MIN < 0 so validation is skipped

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about a missing validation on pad_width_ allowing a negative value (e.g., -5) to be used when computing ngram_width = data_length + 2 * pad_width_, resulting in a negative ngram_width that causes an OOM condition during allocation. The fix adds an OP_REQUIRES check that pad_width_ >= 0. This finding describes a completely different hypothetical integer overflow scenario involving splits_vec_size, which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: Missing validation on pad_width_ allows a negative pad_width (e.g., -5) to produce a negative ngram_width via 'int ngram_width = data_length + 2 * pad_width_', which then causes an out-of-memory condition when used for allocation. The fix adds a check requiring pad_width_ >= 0 in the preserve_short_sequences code path.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalAvgPoolGrad` does not consider cases where the input tensors are invalid allowing an attacker to read from outside of bounds of heap. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: in_row_start is read directly from attacker-controlled row_seq_tensor with no lower-bound validation. If negative, in_r iterates from a negative value, causing in_index to be negative, leading to an out-of-bounds write via in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat.coeffRef(d, in_index).
code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, multiple TensorFlow operations misbehave in eager mode when the resource handle provided to them is invalid. In graph mode, it would have been impossible to perform these API calls, but migration to TF 2.x eager mode opened up this vulnerability. If the resource handle is empty, then a reference is bound to a null pointer inside TensorFlow codebase (various codepaths). This is undefined behavior. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

undesired_operation.description: absl::get<Device*>(op.Device()) will throw absl::bad_variant_access (or call std::terminate in no-exceptions builds) if op.Device() holds an alternative type (e.g., CustomDevice*) instead of Device*, causing a process crash.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash — an attacker who can trigger an eager operation on a CustomDevice with a non-function op and a non-resource, non-host-memory LOCAL tensor handle on a non-CPU device can crash the TensorFlow process.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE-2022-29207 is about an empty resource handle tensor causing a null pointer dereference when accessing tensor->flat<ResourceHandle>()(0) without checking if the tensor has zero elements. The fix adds a check for tensor->NumElements() == 0 before accessing the resource handle. The finding describes a completely different issue (absl::get variant access) unrelated to the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: When a DT_RESOURCE tensor handle contains an empty tensor (zero elements), the code accesses tensor->flat<ResourceHandle>()(0) without checking, leading to undefined behavior (null pointer dereference). The fix adds a check for tensor->NumElements() == 0 and returns an InvalidArgument error.

cve_desc: A Vulnerability of LG Electronic web OS TV Emulator could allow an attacker to escalate privileges and overwrite certain files. This vulnerability is due to wrong environment setting. An attacker could exploit this vulnerability through crafted configuration files and executable files.

undesired_operation.description: When argc == 9 and argv[8] equals "*", arg_start is set to 9. Then argv[arg_start] (i.e., argv[9]) is dereferenced at argv[arg_start][0], which is an out-of-bounds read since valid indices are 0..8.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read that could cause a crash (denial of service) or information disclosure. An attacker on an IRC server can craft a WHO reply to trigger this.

argc is 9, which is greater than 8, so we enter the block

argv[arg_start][0] is accessed, i.e., argv[9][0]. Since argc is 9, valid indices are 0..8, so argv[9] is out of bounds.

actual_issue_summary: When a malformed IRC 352 (WHO) message has exactly 9 arguments with argv[8]=="*", arg_start is set to 9 without checking that argv[9] exists, causing an out-of-bounds array access and crash. The fix adds an (argc > 9) check before setting arg_start to 9.

cve_desc: Buffer overflow in the send_control_msg function in hw/char/virtio-serial-bus.c in QEMU before 2.4.0 allows guest users to cause a denial of service (QEMU process crash) via a crafted virtio control message.

undesired_operation.impact: A malicious guest can trigger a host-side heap buffer overflow, potentially leading to code execution in the host (VM escape) or denial of service.

The fix replaces memcpy with iov_from_buf which properly handles scatter-gather lists and their lengths. The CWE-787 (out-of-bounds write) matches the NVD classification.

A guest can provide a small buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. The fix replaces memcpy with iov_from_buf which correctly handles scatter-gather buffer lists.

cve_desc: Insufficient boundary checks when formatting numbers in number_format allows read/write access to out-of-bounds memory, potentially leading to remote code execution. This issue affects HHVM versions prior to 3.30.10, all versions between 4.0.0 and 4.8.5, all versions between 4.9.0 and 4.18.2, and versions 4.19.0, 4.19.1, 4.20.0, 4.20.1, 4.20.2, 4.21.0, 4.22.0, 4.23.0.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in the computation of 'integral' when accounting for thousand separators. The multiplication thousand_sep.size() * ((integral-1) / 3) produces a size_t value that exceeds INT_MAX. When assigned back to the int variable 'integral', it is truncated, producing a much smaller value. This causes 'reslen' to be undersized, leading to a small heap buffer allocation. The subsequent loop writing digits and thousand separators overflows this buffer via memcpy calls.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow leading to potential arbitrary code execution. An attacker who controls the thousand_sep parameter can cause a large write past the end of the allocated result buffer.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2019-11929) is about incorrect handling of snprintf's return value. When snprintf returns a value >= 64 (meaning the output was truncated), but the first character is not a digit (e.g., '-' for negative numbers), the code used tmplen (the would-have-been-written length) to set the string size via tmpstr.setSize(tmplen), even though only 63 characters were actually written. This caused out-of-bounds access. The fix adds 'tmplen < 64' to the condition check. The finding describes a hypothetical integer overflow in thousand separator computation, which is a completely different issue from the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: When snprintf returns a value >= 64 (indicating truncation), and the first byte of tmpbuf is not a digit (e.g., '-' for negative numbers), the vulnerable code falls through to tmpstr.setSize(tmplen) where tmplen exceeds the actual buffer size of 64 bytes. This causes out-of-bounds memory access. The fix adds a 'tmplen < 64' guard so that the early-return path for non-digit first characters is only taken when the buffer was not truncated, allowing the code to properly reallocate a larger buffer.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseTensorSliceDataset` has an undefined behavior: under certain condition it can be made to dereference a `nullptr` value. The 3 input arguments to `SparseTensorSliceDataset` represent a sparse tensor. However, there are some preconditions that these arguments must satisfy but these are not validated in the implementation. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

for (int64_t i = 0; i < indices->dim_size(0); ++i) {

undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds memory read. An attacker providing a crafted sparse tensor with indices of shape [N, 0] can read memory beyond the indices buffer, potentially causing a crash (denial of service) or information disclosure.

The fix adds checks including `dense_shape->shape().dim_size(0) == indices->shape().dim_size(1)` and `dense_shape->NumElements() > 0`, which together prevent the scenario where indices has 0 columns. This is part of the incomplete precondition validation described in the CVE, which can lead to undefined behavior including nullptr dereference or OOB access.

actual_issue_summary: The SparseTensorSliceDataset implementation had incomplete validation of its sparse tensor inputs. Missing checks included: values count must match first dimension of indices, dense_shape dimensions must match second dimension of indices, and dense_shape must have at least one element. Without these checks, malformed inputs could cause undefined behavior including nullptr dereference.

cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

undesired_operation.description: memcpy writes 'items' elements starting at array->start[start], where start was computed using a stale 'length' value. A side effect during njs_value_to_integer (e.g., valueOf callback on args[1]) can shrink the array to length 0, but start remains large (based on stale length). njs_array_expand only ensures capacity for array->length + delta (= 0 + delta), which is far less than start + items. This causes a heap buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow allowing an attacker to write attacker-controlled values (the splice insertion arguments) past the end of the array buffer, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about uninitialized elements in the 'deleted' fast array when 'this' is not a fast array but has gaps. The fix adds an else branch to set deleted->start[i] to invalid when a property is missing, preventing a segfault in njs_value_own_enumerate when later enumerating the deleted array. The finding describes a completely different speculative issue about stale length values and heap buffer overflow via memcpy, which is not what the CVE or the fix addresses.

actual_issue_summary: When Array.prototype.splice() is called with a non-fast-array 'this' that has gaps (missing properties), the 'deleted' result array (which may be a fast array) is left with uninitialized elements at gap positions. Later enumeration of these uninitialized elements (e.g., in njs_value_own_enumerate) causes a segmentation fault. The fix initializes those gap positions with njs_set_invalid().

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.StagePeek` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `index` is a scalar but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

undesired_operation.description: A negative int value from user input is implicitly converted to a very large std::size_t value when assigned to 'index'. This large value is then passed to buf->Peek(), potentially causing an out-of-bounds read/access.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential out-of-bounds memory access. An attacker providing a negative integer input causes index to wrap to a very large unsigned value (e.g., -1 becomes 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF on 64-bit), which is passed to buf->Peek().

state.value: very large value due to signed-to-unsigned conversion of negative input

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled tensor with negative scalar int value

buf->Peek is called with the wrapped large index

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about missing validation that the input tensor is a scalar before calling .scalar<int>()(). If a non-scalar tensor is provided, the .scalar() call triggers a CHECK-failure crash. The fix adds a shape check (TensorShapeUtils::IsScalar). The finding instead describes a signed-to-unsigned integer conversion issue, which is a different problem from the one the CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: The StagePeek op calls ctx->input(0).scalar<int>()() without first validating that the input tensor is actually a scalar. If a non-scalar tensor is provided, this triggers a CHECK-failure (assertion crash), causing denial of service. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES(ctx, TensorShapeUtils::IsScalar(ctx->input(0).shape()), ...) before accessing the scalar value.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of division in TFLite is [vulnerable to a division by 0 error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/div.cc). There is no check that the divisor tensor does not contain zero elements. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 1e206baedf8bef0334cca3eb92bab134ef525a28. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The return value of EvalDiv<kernel_type>() is discarded. If EvalDiv encounters an error (e.g., division by zero, memory issue), the error is silently ignored and the function returns kTfLiteOk, indicating success. In contrast, the EvalQuantized path correctly checks the return value via TF_LITE_ENSURE_OK.
code_snippets: EvalDiv<kernel_type>(context, node, params, data, input1, input2, output);
undesired_operation.impact: An error condition (such as division by zero with INT32 inputs) in EvalDiv is silently ignored. The caller receives kTfLiteOk and proceeds to use the output tensor which may contain undefined/garbage data, leading to incorrect computation results or potentially further undefined behavior downstream.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-37683) is specifically about the missing check that the divisor tensor does not contain zero elements before performing division (CWE-369, division by zero). The fix adds explicit zero-element checks on input2 for INT32 and UINT8 types before calling EvalDiv/EvalQuantized. The finding instead claims the issue is an unchecked return value from EvalDiv (CWE-252). While the finding mentions division by zero as a possible error, the actual vulnerability is the absence of a pre-division zero check on the divisor tensor, not the unchecked return value. EvalDiv itself does not return an error on division by zero — the division simply causes undefined behavior or a crash. The fix doesn't add return value checking; it adds input validation. This is a different characterization of the problem.

actual_issue_summary: The TFLite division operator (div.cc) did not check whether the divisor tensor (input2) contained zero elements before performing division. For INT32 and UINT8 types, dividing by zero causes undefined behavior. The fix adds explicit checks that all elements of input2 are non-zero before performing the division for INT32 and UINT8 types.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can read from outside of bounds of heap allocated data by sending specially crafted illegal arguments to `tf.raw_ops.SdcaOptimizerV2`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/sdca_internal.cc#L320-L353) does not check that the length of `example_labels` is the same as the number of examples. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit a4e138660270e7599793fa438cd7b2fc2ce215a6. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

If example_labels tensor has fewer elements than example_weights, the access example_labels(example_id) reads out of bounds.

When example_labels is shorter, the loop accessing example_labels(example_id) reads out of bounds from heap memory. The fix adds a check that example_labels.size() == num_examples.

cve_desc: Memory leak in the afra_box_read function in MP4Box in GPAC 1.0.1 allows attackers to read memory via a crafted file.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about memory leaks when ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE fails mid-loop: allocated entries (ae) are not yet added to the list, so they cannot be freed during cleanup. The fix moves gf_list_insert before the ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE calls so that if an error occurs, the already-allocated entry is in the list and will be properly freed. The finding describes a different issue (missing bounds check on global_entry_count) which is not what the CVE or the fix addresses.

actual_issue_summary: Memory leak in afra_box_read: when ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE fails (returns error due to insufficient remaining box size), allocated GF_AfraEntry and GF_GlobalAfraEntry objects are leaked because they haven't been inserted into their respective lists yet. The fix moves gf_list_insert calls to immediately after allocation (before ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE), ensuring entries are tracked in lists and properly freed on error/cleanup.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of SVDF in TFLite is [vulnerable to a null pointer error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/svdf.cc#L300-L313). The [`GetVariableInput` function](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L115-L119) can return a null pointer but `GetTensorData` assumes that the argument is always a valid tensor. Furthermore, because `GetVariableInput` calls [`GetMutableInput`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L82-L90) which might return `nullptr`, the `tensor->is_variable` expression can also trigger a null pointer exception. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5b048e87e4e55990dae6b547add4dae59f4e1c76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: tensor is dereferenced (tensor->is_variable) without a null check after GetMutableInput, which can return nullptr. This leads to a null pointer dereference.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (segmentation fault) when GetMutableInput returns nullptr due to an invalid index or missing input.

out_state.value: crash / undefined behavior due to null pointer dereference

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the null pointer dereference in GetVariableInput where tensor->is_variable is accessed without checking if tensor is nullptr after GetMutableInput. This is exactly the vulnerability described in CVE-2021-37681 and fixed by the commit adding the null check.

actual_issue_summary: GetVariableInput dereferences the pointer returned by GetMutableInput (via tensor->is_variable) without first checking if it is nullptr. The fix adds a null check before the dereference.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in GPAC version 0.8.0 and 1.0.1. There is an invalid pointer dereference in the function SetupWriters() in isomedia/isom_store.c.

undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via memory exhaustion.

The fix adds null checks for these fields before they are dereferenced. The finding describes a memory leak on allocation failure, which is a different issue entirely and not what the CVE addresses.

The fix adds validation checks for these pointers before they are dereferenced, returning GF_ISOM_INVALID_FILE if any are NULL.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: c->Dim(input, in_idx) is called with a negative in_idx value. The check 'if (in_idx >= rank)' only validates the upper bound but does not check for negative values. Since in_idx is derived from attacker-controlled perm tensor data, a negative value passes the bounds check and causes an out-of-bounds read in c->Dim().
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read or crash — attacker provides a negative permutation index that bypasses the upper-bound check, potentially reading out-of-bounds memory or causing a denial of service.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the CVE: negative perm values bypass the upper-bound-only check (in_idx >= rank), leading to out-of-bounds access in c->Dim(). The fix adds the missing lower-bound check (in_idx <= -rank). This matches the CVE description exactly.

actual_issue_summary: The Transpose shape inference function only checked that perm indices were less than rank (upper bound) but did not validate negative indices. A negative perm value would pass the check and be used as an index to c->Dim(), causing a heap buffer overflow/OOB read. The fix adds a lower-bound check: in_idx <= -rank.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: out_flat(idx) writes to an attacker-controlled index without bounds checking. The idx is computed from sparse tensor group coordinates via CoordinatesToFlatIndex, and if the sparse tensor indices are out of the declared shape bounds (negative or too large), idx can be out of bounds for the output tensor.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds write leading to heap corruption, potential arbitrary code execution, or denial of service via crash.

Loop iterates over sparse tensor groups; CoordinatesToFlatIndex computes idx from group coordinates and strides

judgments.reasoning: The finding directly identifies the same vulnerability as CVE-2021-37635: the missing bounds check on idx before writing to out_flat(idx), which allows heap OOB access. The fix adds exactly these bounds checks. While the CVE description mentions CWE-125 (OOB read) and the finding says CWE-787 (OOB write), the write is the core issue fixed by the patch, so this is correct.

actual_issue_summary: In sparse reduction operations, the computed flat index (idx) from CoordinatesToFlatIndex was used to write to the output tensor without validating that (1) group size matches output_strides size and (2) idx is within bounds of the output tensor. This allows heap out-of-bounds access when malicious sparse tensor indices are provided. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks for both conditions.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.SparseReshape` can be made to trigger an integral division by 0 exception. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L176-L181) calls the reshaping functor whenever there is at least an index in the input but does not check that shape of the input or the target shape have both a non-zero number of elements. The [reshape functor](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L40-L78) blindly divides by the dimensions of the target shape. Hence, if this is not checked, code will result in a division by 0. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4923de56ec94fff7770df259ab7f2288a74feb41. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1 as this is the other affected version.
diff:
--- vulnerable
+++ fixed
@@ -92,6 +92,12 @@
                                           TensorShape({nnz, output_rank}),
                                           &result_indices));
   if (nnz > 0) {
+    OP_REQUIRES(
+        context, dense_size > 0 && product > 0,
+        errors::InvalidArgument(
+            "Input tensor has ", nnz, " non zero elements but input shape (",
+            input_shape.DebugString(), ") or output shape (",
+            output_shape.DebugString(), ") is empty"));
     OP_REQUIRES_OK(context, functor::ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor<Device>()(
                                 context, input_shape, output_shape,
                                 input_indices_in.matrix<int64>(),

undesired_operation.description: Signed integer overflow in 'product *= size' where product accumulates the multiplication of attacker-controlled dimension sizes from target_shape. With carefully chosen dimension values, the product can overflow int64_t and wrap to a small positive value, causing subsequent checks (product > 0, product * missing == dense_size, output_shape.num_elements() == dense_size) to pass with an incorrect output_shape whose true element count does not match dense_size. This leads to incorrect index computations in ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor, potentially causing out-of-bounds memory access.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential out-of-bounds memory access. An attacker can craft target_shape dimensions that cause signed integer overflow, bypassing validation checks and leading to a malformed output_shape used in subsequent index computations.

state.value: overflowed int64_t value wrapping to small positive

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop iterates over target_shape dimensions, multiplying each into product

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2021-37640) is about a division by zero in the ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor when the output shape has a zero dimension but nnz > 0. The fix adds a check that dense_size > 0 && product > 0 before calling the functor. The finding describes a hypothetical integer overflow in product *= size, which is a different issue entirely and not what the CVE or the fix addresses.

actual_issue_summary: Division by zero in ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor when SparseReshape is called with nnz > 0 but the output shape contains a zero dimension (product == 0) or the input shape has zero elements (dense_size == 0). The fix adds a check that both dense_size > 0 and product > 0 before calling the reshape functor.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the code for sparse matrix multiplication is vulnerable to undefined behavior via binding a reference to `nullptr`. This occurs whenever the dimensions of `a` or `b` are 0 or less. In the case on one of these is 0, an empty output tensor should be allocated (to conserve the invariant that output tensors are always allocated when the operation is successful) but nothing should be written to it (that is, we should return early from the kernel implementation). Otherwise, attempts to write to this empty tensor would result in heap OOB access. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: Tensor dimension values returned by dim_size() (int64) are truncated when stored into int variables (m, k, n, k2). This can cause the k == k2 check to pass with wrong values, and the output tensor to be allocated with incorrect (smaller) dimensions via TensorShape({m, n}). The subsequent matrix multiplication writes results based on actual (large) tensor dimensions into the undersized output buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.
code_snippets: const int m = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(1) : a.dim_size(0);
const int k = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(0) : a.dim_size(1);
const int n = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(0) : b.dim_size(1);
const int k2 = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(1) : b.dim_size(0);
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow leading to potential arbitrary code execution or denial of service. An attacker who controls input tensor shapes can craft dimensions that overflow when truncated to int, causing a small output buffer to be allocated while the matrix multiply writes far more data.

attacker-controlled matrix tensor with large int64 dimensions

out_state.value: truncated to int, potentially small or negative
truncated to int, potentially small or negative

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about missing validation for zero or negative matrix dimensions (m, n, k), leading to heap OOB access when writing to an empty output tensor. The fix adds checks for non-negative dimensions and early return when m==0 or n==0. The finding instead describes an integer truncation issue from int64 to int, which is a different class of vulnerability not addressed by the fix. The actual CVE doesn't involve integer overflow/truncation - it's about zero-dimension matrices causing undefined behavior and heap OOB writes.

actual_issue_summary: When matrix dimensions m or n are 0, the code allocates an empty output tensor but proceeds to write to it (e.g., via matrix multiplication kernels), causing heap out-of-bounds access. The fix adds validation that dimensions are non-negative and returns early when output dimensions are zero.

cve_desc: adts_decode_extradata in libavformat/adtsenc.c in FFmpeg 4.4 does not check the init_get_bits return value, which is a necessary step because the second argument to init_get_bits can be crafted.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in 'size * 8' when computing bit count for init_get_bits. When size > INT_MAX/8, the multiplication overflows, producing a negative or incorrect value. The return value of init_get_bits is not checked, so the function proceeds to use the improperly initialized GetBitContext, leading to out-of-bounds reads via get_bits() calls.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read or use of improperly initialized GetBitContext, potentially leading to information disclosure or crash

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the CVE: the unchecked return value of init_get_bits (CWE-252) combined with the integer overflow in size*8 (CWE-190) that can cause init_get_bits to fail with crafted input. The fix addresses exactly this by switching to init_get_bits8 and checking the return value.

undesired_operation.description: When channel_conf is 0, ff_copy_pce_data reads PCE element data from the GetBitContext which may have very few bits remaining relative to what PCE parsing requires. This causes out-of-bounds reads past the end of buf, as the PCE format contains variable-length fields that can require many more bits than remain in the small input buffer.

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes insufficient bounds checking in ff_copy_pce_data when reading PCE data, which is a separate issue from the CVE. The CVE is specifically about not checking the return value of init_get_bits when the size*8 argument can be crafted to cause failure. The fix doesn't address PCE parsing bounds - it only adds the return value check for init_get_bits.

actual_issue_summary: The function adts_decode_extradata calls init_get_bits(&gb, buf, size * 8) without checking its return value. Since buf and size come from potentially crafted input, the size*8 multiplication can overflow, causing init_get_bits to fail. Without checking this failure, the function proceeds to use an improperly initialized GetBitContext. The fix replaces init_get_bits with init_get_bits8 (avoiding the manual *8 multiplication) and adds a return value check.

cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.

undesired_operation.description: strncmp is used to compare binary hash digests. strncmp stops comparing at the first null byte ('\0'). If the stored password hash (passwd2_sum/key) contains a null byte early in the digest, strncmp will only compare bytes before that null byte, drastically reducing the effective comparison length and making brute-force trivial.
undesired_operation.impact: Authentication bypass — an attacker can find a password whose hash matches the stored hash on only the bytes before the first null byte. For a hash with a null byte at position 1, only 1 byte needs to match, which is trivially brute-forceable (1 in 256 chance per guess).

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-37848) is specifically about timing side-channel information leakage because strncmp is used for hash comparison, allowing an attacker to deduce hash contents by measuring comparison time (CWE-200). The finding describes a different issue: null-byte truncation in strncmp (CWE-187), which would weaken the hash comparison length. While both problems stem from using strncmp on binary hash data and are fixed by the same code change, the finding describes a fundamentally different attack vector (brute-force due to shortened comparison) than the CVE (timing side-channel leakage). The CVE description explicitly states 'leaks timing information' as the vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a timing side-channel in password hash comparison. strncmp was used to compare password hashes, which returns early on the first differing byte, leaking timing information that allows an attacker to progressively guess the correct hash byte-by-byte. The fix replaces strncmp with crypto_memneq, which performs constant-time comparison.

cve_desc: In Das U-Boot through 2022.07-rc5, an integer signedness error and resultant stack-based buffer overflow in the "i2c md" command enables the corruption of the return address pointer of the do_i2c_md function.

undesired_operation.description: When length (uint) exceeds INT_MAX, the assignment nbytes = length causes signed integer overflow making nbytes negative. Then linebytes = min(nbytes, DISP_LINE_LEN) also becomes negative. This negative linebytes is passed to i2c_read/dm_i2c_read as the read length. If the underlying function casts this to size_t or unsigned, it becomes a very large value, causing a massive read into the stack buffer linebuf[DISP_LINE_LEN].
code_snippets: ret = i2c_read(chip, addr, alen, linebuf, linebytes);
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to potential code execution or denial of service. An attacker who can control command arguments can overflow the stack buffer linebuf.

length is assigned to signed int nbytes, causing integer signedness conversion overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: linebytes is assigned nbytes (the false branch of the ternary)
i2c_read or dm_i2c_read is called with linebuf (16-byte stack buffer) and linebytes (-2147483392), which when interpreted as unsigned short by some drivers becomes 256, overflowing the 16-byte buffer

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely describes CVE-2022-34835: the integer signedness error where unsigned length is assigned to signed nbytes, causing the comparison with DISP_LINE_LEN to fail, resulting in a large/negative linebytes value passed to i2c_read, overflowing the 16-byte stack buffer. The CWEs (CWE-120 buffer overflow, CWE-681 integer signedness) align with the CVE's CWE-787 (out-of-bounds write).

actual_issue_summary: Integer signedness error in do_i2c_md: the unsigned 'length' parameter is assigned to signed 'int nbytes', causing values >= 0x80000000 to become negative. This makes the comparison 'nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN' false, so linebytes gets the large negative value instead of being clamped to 16. The negative linebytes is then passed to i2c_read/dm_i2c_read, which can overflow the 16-byte stack buffer linebuf. The fix changes nbytes, linebytes, j, and alen from int to uint.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble` can result in a use after free error if an attacker supplies specially crafted arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/resource_ops.cc#L55) uses a reference counted resource and decrements the refcount if the initialization fails, as it should. However, when the code was written, the resource was represented as a naked pointer but later refactoring has changed it to be a smart pointer. Thus, when the pointer leaves the scope, a subsequent `free`-ing of the resource occurs, but this fails to take into account that the refcount has already reached 0, thus the resource has been already freed. During this double-free process, members of the resource object are accessed for cleanup but they are invalid as the entire resource has been freed. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5ecec9c6fbdbc6be03295685190a45e7eee726ab. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: When InitFromSerialized fails, result->Unref() is called which decrements the reference count to 0 and deletes the BoostedTreesEnsembleResource object. Then OP_REQUIRES triggers a return, causing the unique_ptr destructor to call delete on the same already-freed pointer, resulting in a double-free.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential code execution through heap corruption. An attacker can trigger this by providing a malformed tree_ensemble_serialized proto that fails parsing.

state.value: unique_ptr holding pointer to already-freed BoostedTreesEnsembleResource after Unref() decrements refcount to 0

out_state.value: unique_ptr owning a valid BoostedTreesEnsembleResource with refcount=1

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Attacker supplies a malformed tree_ensemble_serialized proto that fails parsing, so InitFromSerialized returns false
