judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches CVE-2022-31031: unbounded writes to the msg->attr array due to missing bounds check on attr_count in the while loop, which is a stack buffer overflow (CWE-787). The fix adds the missing `msg->attr_count < attr_max_cnt` check.

actual_issue_summary: In pjstun_parse_msg(), the while loop parsing STUN attributes increments msg->attr_count and writes to msg->attr[] without checking whether attr_count has exceeded the fixed array size. A crafted STUN message with many small attributes causes a stack buffer overflow. The fix adds a bounds check (msg->attr_count < attr_max_cnt) to the loop condition.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. PJSIP versions 2.12 and prior do not parse incoming RTCP feedback RPSI (Reference Picture Selection Indication) packet, but any app that directly uses pjmedia_rtcp_fb_parse_rpsi() will be affected. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

undesired_operation.description: When hdr->length (after ntohs) is 0 or 1, the expression (pj_ntohs(hdr->length)-2) underflows as a signed int, producing -2 or -1. Multiplied by 4 gives -8 or -4, which when stored into the unsigned pj_size_t rpsi_len wraps to a near-maximum value. This huge rpsi_len propagates to rpsi->rpsi_bit_len and pj_strset length, enabling out-of-bounds reads.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure via out-of-bounds read when caller processes rpsi->rpsi using the attacker-influenced, massively inflated length. Potential denial of service via crash.

state.value: near-maximum unsigned value due to underflow wrap

initial_state.value: size of the buffer, at least sizeof(pjmedia_rtcp_common)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Both pointers are non-null
Caller provides buffer at least as large as the common header
Attacker crafts packet with pt=RTCP_PSFB and count=3

step_by_step_execution.description: rpsi_len is computed from hdr->length. With hdr->length=0 after ntohs, (0-2)*4 = -8 as signed, which wraps to 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF8 on 64-bit when stored in pj_size_t

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: rpsi_len+12 = 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF8+12 = 4 (unsigned overflow wraps). length >= sizeof(pjmedia_rtcp_common) which is >= 4, so the check passes.

step_by_step_execution.description: padlen is read from buffer byte after header
rpsi_bit_len computed as rpsi_len*8 - 16 - padlen, producing a huge value, then pj_strset sets rpsi->rpsi to point into the buffer with this enormous length

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the integer underflow when hdr->length < 2, which is exactly what the fix addresses by adding the check 'hdr->rtcp_common.length < 3'. This leads to OOB read, matching CVE-2022-24786's description of the RTCP FB RPSI parsing vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerable function pjmedia_rtcp_fb_parse_rpsi() lacks validation of hdr->length (must be >= 3) and padlen (must be < 32 and consistent with rpsi_len). When hdr->length is small (0, 1, or 2), the subtraction (hdr->length - 2) underflows, producing a huge rpsi_len that bypasses the length check due to unsigned overflow, leading to out-of-bounds read/write. Similarly, an excessively large padlen can cause rpsi_bit_len underflow. The fix adds three new validation checks to prevent these issues.

cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.
diff:
--- vulnerable
+++ fixed
@@ -28,7 +28,11 @@
 		return MP_OUT_OF_MEMORY;
 	
 	header->insnum = f.readWord(); // number of instruments
-	header->patnum = f.readWord(); // number of patterns	
+	if (header->insnum > MP_MAXINS)
+		return MP_LOADER_FAILED;
+	header->patnum = f.readWord(); // number of patterns
+	if (header->patnum > 256)
+		return MP_LOADER_FAILED;
 	
 	mp_sint32 flags = f.readWord(); // st3 flags

undesired_operation.description: header->insnum is read from the file as a 16-bit word and used as the loop bound 'i < header->insnum'. Inside the loop, instr[i] and smp[s] are accessed, where instr and smp are pre-allocated arrays with fixed maximum sizes. If insnum exceeds this maximum, out-of-bounds writes occur.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — attacker controls header->insnum via crafted S3M file, enabling arbitrary writes to heap memory past the instr[] and smp[] arrays.

initial_state.value: pre-allocated array of fixed size MP_MAXINS

step_by_step_execution.description: header->insnum is read from the file as a 16-bit word

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: In the vulnerable version there is no bounds check on insnum after reading it, so any value up to 65535 is accepted

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop iterates with i from 0 to header->insnum-1, accessing instr[i] and smp[s] which are beyond allocated bounds

out_state.value: up to 65534, causing out-of-bounds access on instr[] and smp[]

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly matches the CVE fix which adds a bounds check 'if (header->insnum > MP_MAXINS) return MP_LOADER_FAILED;' to prevent heap overflow from an unchecked insnum value.

undesired_operation.description: header->patnum is read from the file as a 16-bit word and used as the loop bound for 'i < header->patnum'. If patnum exceeds the fixed maximum (256), out-of-bounds access occurs on phead[].
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — attacker controls header->patnum via crafted S3M file, enabling out-of-bounds writes past the phead[] array.

step_by_step_execution.description: header->patnum is read from the file as a 16-bit word

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: In the vulnerable version there is no bounds check on patnum after reading it, so any value up to 65535 is accepted

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop iterates with i from 0 to header->patnum-1, accessing phead[i] beyond its allocated bounds

out_state.value: up to 999, causing out-of-bounds access on phead[]

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly matches the second part of the CVE fix which adds 'if (header->patnum > 256) return MP_LOADER_FAILED;' to prevent heap overflow from an unchecked patnum value.

actual_issue_summary: The S3M loader reads header->insnum and header->patnum from the file without validating them against maximum bounds (MP_MAXINS and 256 respectively). A crafted S3M file with excessively large values causes heap buffer overflows when these values are used as loop bounds to access pre-allocated arrays (instr[], smp[], phead[]). The fix adds bounds checks immediately after reading these values.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

undesired_operation.description: When axis_ != -1 and input_min_tensor is a scalar (dims() == 0), calling input_min_tensor.dim_size(0) accesses dimension index 0 of a tensor with no dimensions, causing an out-of-bounds read on the internal shape/dimension array.
code_snippets:
OP_REQUIRES(
          ctx, input_min_tensor.dim_size(0) == depth,
          errors::InvalidArgument("min has incorrect size, expected ", depth,
                                  " was ", input_min_tensor.dim_size(0)));

OP_REQUIRES(
          ctx, input_max_tensor.dim_size(0) == depth,
          errors::InvalidArgument("max has incorrect size, expected ", depth,
                                  " was ", input_max_tensor.dim_size(0)));
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential information disclosure from reading out-of-bounds memory in the tensor shape metadata.

step_by_step_execution.description: axis_ is checked to be >= -1 and less than input dims
input_min_tensor.dims() == 0 passes the dims check (0 || 1)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: axis_ was set to a non-negative value

step_by_step_execution.description: input_min_tensor.dim_size(0) is called on a 0-dimensional tensor

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is specifically about the axis_ == -1 code path where input_min and input_max are used as scalars via .scalar<T>() without validating they are actually scalars. The PoC demonstrates passing a 1-D tensor with shape [0] as input_min when axis=-1, triggering a CHECK failure in .scalar<T>(). The finding describes a different code path (axis_ != -1) and a different issue (dim_size(0) on a 0-D tensor), which is not what the CVE fix addresses. The fix adds IsScalar checks in the axis_ == -1 branch, not in the axis_ != -1 branch.

actual_issue_summary: When axis_ == -1, the code calls .scalar<T>() on input_min_tensor and input_max_tensor without verifying they are actually scalars. A 1-D tensor (e.g., shape [0]) passes the dims() == 0 || dims() == 1 validation but causes a CHECK failure when .scalar<T>() is called. The fix adds explicit IsScalar checks before the scalar access in the axis_ == -1 branch.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The pointer 'dequeue_node' is declared but never initialized. If the loop over train_nodes finds no node satisfying IsDequeueOp(), dequeue_node remains uninitialized. It is then read in 'if (dequeue_node)', which is undefined behavior. If the uninitialized value happens to be non-null, dequeue_node->name() dereferences a garbage pointer.
undesired_operation.impact: If no dequeue op exists in the graph, reading the uninitialized pointer is undefined behavior. If the garbage value is non-null, dereferencing it (dequeue_node->name()) leads to a crash or potential code execution.

initial_state.value: empty vector, to be populated by ComputeTransitiveFanin

step_by_step_execution.description: dequeue_node is declared as a raw pointer without initialization
Loop iterates over train_nodes looking for a Dequeue op node

in_state.value: vector of NodeDef* with no Dequeue op nodes

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: dequeue_node is uninitialized; if its garbage value is non-zero, the condition evaluates to true

relevant_state.value: uninitialized garbage, happens to be non-null

step_by_step_execution.description: dequeue_node->name() is called, dereferencing the garbage pointer

out_state.value: undefined behavior: reading memory at arbitrary address

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-41225: the use of an uninitialized variable dequeue_node in Grappler's AutoParallel::Initialize, which the fix addresses by initializing it to nullptr.

actual_issue_summary: In AutoParallel::Initialize, the pointer 'dequeue_node' was declared without initialization. If no Dequeue node exists in train_nodes, the pointer remains uninitialized and is subsequently used in a conditional check and potentially dereferenced, causing undefined behavior. The fix initializes it to nullptr.

cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds read on bin->segment_entries array using attacker-controlled segnum value with no upper bounds check in the moveable bundle case
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure or crash — attacker can read out-of-bounds memory from the segment_entries array by crafting a NE binary with a large segnum value in the entry table

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop iterates through entry table bundles, reading bundle_length and bundle_type

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the missing upper bounds check on segnum in the moveable bundle case. The fix adds exactly this check: `segnum < bin->ne_header->SegCount`. This is a core component of CVE-2022-1297 (out-of-bounds read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints).

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2022-1297 involves multiple out-of-bounds reads in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints: (1) missing upper bounds check on segnum allowing OOB read of segment_entries in the moveable bundle case, (2) missing bounds check on off before reading from entry_table in both moveable and fixed bundle cases, and (3) unaligned pointer casts (ut16*) replaced with safe r_read_le16 calls.

cve_desc: Exim 4.72 and earlier allows local users to gain privileges by leveraging the ability of the exim user account to specify an alternate configuration file with a directive that contains arbitrary commands, as demonstrated by the spool_directory directive.

+privileges. */

   {
   if (fstat(fileno(config_file), &statbuf) != 0)
     log_write(0, LOG_MAIN|LOG_PANIC_DIE, "failed to stat configuration file %s",

undesired_operation.description: sprintf writes past the end of big_buffer when appending node name and euid suffix to filename. suffix points to the end of filename within big_buffer, and sprintf appends up to ~279 additional bytes which can overflow big_buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack or heap buffer overflow — if filename nearly fills big_buffer, appending up to ~279 characters via sprintf overflows the buffer, potentially enabling code execution

state.value: points to end of filename within big_buffer, near the end of big_buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: filename is read from config_main_filelist into big_buffer via string_nextinlist

out_state.value: points to big_buffer, containing a path string

step_by_step_execution.description: suffix is set to end of filename within big_buffer

out_state.value: filename + Ustrlen(filename), pointing to null terminator in big_buffer

in_state.value: points near end of big_buffer if filename is long

out_state.value: overflowed if filename + suffix content exceeds big_buffer_size

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential buffer overflow in sprintf, which is unrelated to CVE-2010-4345. The actual CVE is a local privilege escalation where the permission check on configuration files is skipped when an alternate config file is specified via command line (the `!config_changed` condition). The fix changes the condition to `trusted_config` so that file permissions are always checked when running with root privileges, regardless of whether the config file was changed.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerable code skips configuration file permission checks when `config_changed` is true (i.e., when an alternate configuration file is specified on the command line). This allows the exim user to specify a malicious configuration file containing arbitrary commands (e.g., via spool_directory) without the file ownership/permission checks being applied. The fix changes the condition from `!config_changed` to `trusted_config`, ensuring permission checks are performed whenever the process retains root privileges.

cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

undesired_operation.description: When instr[y].size is read from the file as a value less than 4 (e.g., 0, 1, 2, or 3), the expression 'instr[y].size - 4' underflows because both operands are unsigned 32-bit integers. This produces a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFE for size=2), which is passed as the length to f.read(), causing a massive read into a 29-byte stack buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to arbitrary code execution. An attacker crafting a malicious XM file with an instrument header size < 4 can overwrite the stack.

state.value: large unsigned value due to underflow (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFC for size=0)

initial_state.value: unset, will be read from file
0x104 (to reach the v1.04 instrument loading path)

step_by_step_execution.description: instr[y].size is read from the XM file

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: With instr[y].size = 2, 2 < 29 is true, so we enter the branch that reads into a local buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: f.read(buffer, 1, instr[y].size - 4) is called. Since instr[y].size is an unsigned 32-bit integer with value 2, subtracting 4 causes unsigned integer underflow producing 0xFFFFFFFE.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches the CVE-2022-34927 vulnerability. The fix adds a check `instr[y].size >= 4` to prevent the unsigned integer underflow in `instr[y].size - 4` that leads to a stack buffer overflow when writing into the 29-byte local buffer. The finding correctly identifies the root cause (integer underflow) and the consequence (stack overflow/corruption).

actual_issue_summary: When an XM instrument header claims a size less than 4, the expression `instr[y].size - 4` underflows (unsigned arithmetic) producing a huge value, which is used as the length for f.read() into a 29-byte stack buffer, causing stack corruption. The fix adds a `>= 4` lower bound check.

cve_desc: Integer overflow in the ljpeg_start function in dcraw 7.00 and earlier allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted image, which triggers a buffer overflow, related to the len variable.

undesired_operation.description: In the 0xffda case, data[0] is used as an index multiplier without validation against len. Accessing data[1+data[0]*2] and data[3+data[0]*2] can read beyond the actual bytes read by fread into the data buffer, reading uninitialized stack memory.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure - uninitialized stack data is used to set jh->psv and modify jh->bits, potentially leaking stack contents or causing incorrect behavior

state.value: attacker-controlled value that causes 1+data[0]*2 or 3+data[0]*2 to exceed len

step_by_step_execution.description: fread reads tag and len bytes from file

out_state.value: small value from crafted file, e.g. 2

step_by_step_execution.description: fread reads len bytes into data buffer
data[0] is used to compute index 1+data[0]*2 = 257, which exceeds len=2

out_state.value: reads data[257], which is uninitialized stack memory

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential out-of-bounds read in the 0xffda handler due to unchecked data[0] indexing. However, the actual CVE-2015-3885 is specifically about an integer overflow in the `len` variable: when `len` is declared as `int` and the expression `(data[2] << 8 | data[3]) - 2` produces a negative value (e.g., when data[2]=0 and data[3]=0, len=-2), this negative value is passed to fread as a size_t, causing a massive read into the stack buffer and a buffer overflow/crash. The fix changes `len` from `int` to `ushort` to prevent negative values. The finding describes a completely different issue unrelated to the len integer overflow.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in the `len` variable declared as `int`. When the expression `(data[2] << 8 | data[3]) - 2` evaluates to a negative number (e.g., -2 when both bytes are 0), this negative int is implicitly converted to a very large size_t when passed to fread(), causing a buffer overflow on the stack-allocated `data[0x10000]` buffer. The fix changes `len` from `int` to `ushort`, ensuring it wraps to a positive value within the buffer's bounds.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.16-rc6. There is a lack of check after calling vzalloc() and lack of free after allocation in drivers/media/test-drivers/vidtv/vidtv_s302m.c.

undesired_operation.impact: Memory leak of encoder_buf (VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ bytes) and potentially e->name on each failed initialization, which can lead to denial of service through memory exhaustion.

e->name is allocated via kstrdup if args.name is set

Additionally, the error path when ctx allocation fails does not free e->encoder_buf or e->name, causing memory leaks. The fix adds a NULL check after vzalloc and fixes the error cleanup paths to properly free all allocated resources.

cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the dynamicGetbuf function in gd_io_dp.c in the GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) through 2.2.3, as used in PHP before 5.6.28 and 7.x before 7.0.13, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (stack-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted imagecreatefromstring call.

undesired_operation.description: When dp->pos > dp->logicalSize, 'remain' becomes negative. The check 'remain == 0' does not catch negative values, so rlen is set to the negative remain. When passed to memcpy as a size_t parameter, this negative int is implicitly converted to a very large unsigned value, causing a massive heap buffer over-read.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer over-read leading to denial of service (crash) or information disclosure of heap memory contents

initial_state.value: attacker-influenced value from crafted image data

step_by_step_execution.description: remain is computed as dp->logicalSize - dp->pos

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: remain is negative, len is positive, so remain < len
remain is negative, not zero, so the early return is skipped

step_by_step_execution.description: rlen is assigned the negative remain value
memcpy is called with rlen as size; the negative int is implicitly converted to a very large size_t, causing a stack-based or heap buffer overflow

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core vulnerability: when remain is negative, the 'remain == 0' check fails to catch it, allowing a negative rlen to be passed to memcpy where it's converted to a huge size_t. This is exactly the integer signedness error described in CVE-2016-8670, fixed by changing 'remain == 0' to 'remain <= 0'. The CWEs differ slightly (CWE-119 vs CWE-122/125/681) but describe the same underlying problem.

actual_issue_summary: Integer signedness error in dynamicGetbuf: when dp->pos > dp->logicalSize, 'remain' becomes negative. The check 'remain == 0' does not catch negative values, so the negative remain is assigned to rlen and passed to memcpy, where implicit conversion to size_t causes a stack-based buffer overflow. The fix changes the check from '== 0' to '<= 0'.

cve_desc: A flaw was found in cairo's image-compositor.c in all versions prior to 1.17.4. This flaw allows an attacker who can provide a crafted input file to cairo's image-compositor (for example, by convincing a user to open a file in an application using cairo, or if an application uses cairo on untrusted input) to cause a stack buffer overflow -> out-of-bounds WRITE. The highest impact from this vulnerability is to confidentiality, integrity, as well as system availability.

undesired_operation.description: In the else branch, coverage bytes are written into r->_buf via pointer m with no bounds checking. The total number of bytes written equals the sum of (spans[i+1].x - spans[i].x) for all consecutive spans that enter the else branch without resetting m. If this sum exceeds the allocated size of r->_buf, a heap buffer overflow occurs.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow leading to potential arbitrary code execution or denial of service. An attacker who controls the span data (e.g., via a crafted vector graphic) can write arbitrary coverage bytes past the end of r->_buf.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies CVE-2020-35492: the use of r->_buf as the write target for coverage data instead of the properly-sized mask pixel data buffer (obtained via pixman_image_get_data(r->mask)). The fix replaces r->_buf with the mask's actual data buffer. The finding accurately describes the out-of-bounds write (CWE-787) that occurs when coverage bytes overflow r->_buf. The finding says 'heap' while the CVE says 'stack', but the core vulnerability is correctly identified.

actual_issue_summary: The mask coverage data was being written into r->_buf (a small fixed-size buffer in the renderer struct) instead of the actual mask image pixel data buffer obtained via pixman_image_get_data(r->mask). When span coverage data exceeded the size of r->_buf, an out-of-bounds write (stack buffer overflow) occurred. The fix changes the write target from r->_buf to the mask's properly allocated pixel data buffer.

cve_desc: In OpenLDAP through 2.4.57 and 2.5.x through 2.5.1alpha, an assertion failure in slapd can occur in the issuerAndThisUpdateCheck function via a crafted packet, resulting in a denial of service (daemon exit) via a short timestamp. This is related to schema_init.c and checkTime.

step_by_step_execution.description: Code accesses is->bv_val[is->bv_len+1] which equals is->bv_val[x.bv_len], one past the logical boundary

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-27212) is specifically about a missing length validation on the thisUpdate timestamp field, which allows a short timestamp to pass through and trigger an assertion failure (CWE-617) in the downstream checkTime function. The fix adds `if ( tu->bv_len < STRLENOF("YYYYmmddHHmmssZ") ) return LDAP_INVALID_SYNTAX;`. The finding describes a potential one-byte out-of-bounds read in the issuer scanning loop, which is an entirely different issue unrelated to the short timestamp assertion failure that constitutes the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a missing minimum-length check on the thisUpdate timestamp field parsed in issuerAndThisUpdateCheck. A crafted packet with a short thisUpdate value passes validation and later triggers an assertion failure in the checkTime function, causing slapd to crash (denial of service). The fix adds a check ensuring tu->bv_len is at least the length of a proper timestamp string ("YYYYmmddHHmmssZ").

cve_desc: Array index error in the (1) dtoa implementation in dtoa.c (aka pdtoa.c) and the (2) gdtoa (aka new dtoa) implementation in gdtoa/misc.c in libc, as used in multiple operating systems and products including in FreeBSD 6.4 and 7.2, NetBSD 5.0, OpenBSD 4.5, Mozilla Firefox 3.0.x before 3.0.15 and 3.5.x before 3.5.4, K-Meleon 1.5.3, SeaMonkey 1.1.8, and other products, allows context-dependent attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via a large precision value in the format argument to a printf function, which triggers incorrect memory allocation and a heap-based buffer overflow during conversion to a floating-point number.

undesired_operation.description: When k is large (e.g., >= 31 on systems with 32-bit int), the expression '1 << k' causes signed integer overflow, which is undefined behavior in C. Practically, on two's complement systems, k=31 yields x = INT_MIN (a large negative value). The subsequent MALLOC size computation 'sizeof(Bigint) + (x-1)*sizeof(Long)' underflows/wraps, resulting in a very small allocation. However, rv->maxwds is set to this overflowed x value, so subsequent code that trusts maxwds will write far beyond the allocated buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — an undersized buffer is allocated but maxwds indicates a much larger capacity, allowing subsequent writes to overflow the heap buffer, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE describes an array index error in Balloc where a large precision value leads to incorrect memory allocation and heap-based buffer overflow. The finding identifies the same root cause: when k is too large (no bounds check against Kmax), it leads to integer overflow in '1 << k', incorrect memory allocation, and heap buffer overflow. The fix adds exactly the check 'k > Kmax' that prevents this. While the finding focuses on the integer overflow / heap overflow consequence rather than the initial array index error on freelist[k], it correctly identifies a key component of the same vulnerability chain that the CVE describes.

actual_issue_summary: The Balloc function lacked a bounds check on the parameter k against Kmax, allowing out-of-bounds access to freelist[k] and integer overflow in '1 << k', leading to incorrect memory allocation and heap-based buffer overflow. The fix adds a check 'k > Kmax' and also a NULL check on the MALLOC return value.

cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

undesired_operation.description: Heap buffer overflow when writing nspace_declarations entries. The allocation size is attribute_count+1 (plus declared_nspaces and xml_language), but for each attribute, up to TWO entries are written (one namespace declaration + one attribute value), leading to writes beyond the allocated buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow allows an attacker to corrupt heap memory, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

step_by_step_execution.description: nspace_max_count is calculated as attribute_count+1
nspace_declarations is allocated with nspace_max_count entries

out_state.value: heap buffer of (N+1) * sizeof(struct nsd) bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: For each attribute i (0..N-1), up to 2 entries are written: one for namespace declaration and one for attribute value. This means up to 2*N entries total, plus 1 for element namespace.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: With N=3 attributes each needing namespace declaration, nspace_declarations_count can reach up to 2*3+1=7, but nspace_max_count is only 4, so writes go past the allocated buffer.

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly describes the CVE-2017-18926 vulnerability: the miscalculation of nspace_max_count as attribute_count+1 instead of attribute_count*2 (plus conditionally +1 for element nspace), leading to heap buffer overflow when each attribute can generate two entries in the nspace_declarations array.

actual_issue_summary: The nspace_max_count calculation in raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common was attribute_count+1, but each attribute can produce up to 2 entries (one namespace declaration + one attribute value). The fix changes it to attribute_count*2 and conditionally adds 1 for element->name->nspace, preventing heap buffer overflow.

cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

undesired_operation.description: When groupCode==42 arrives before any groupCode==10, vertexIndex is -1. The code checks 'vertexIndex<maxVertices' but does NOT check 'vertexIndex>=0', leading to an out-of-bounds write at vertices[4*(-1)+3] = vertices[-1].
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer underflow write — attacker writes a controlled double value to the memory location just before the vertices buffer, potentially corrupting heap metadata or adjacent objects.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: groupCode is 42, not 90, so we skip the allocation block
groupCode is 42, so this branch is entered
groupCode is 42, not 10, so vertexIndex is not incremented
42 > 30, so we go to the else-if branch
groupCode is 42 and vertexIndex (-1) < maxVertices (>0), so this condition is true. The missing check for vertexIndex>=0 allows negative index access.

step_by_step_execution.description: Write to vertices[4*(-1)+3] = vertices[-1] with attacker-controlled double value

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely identifies the vulnerability fixed by the commit: the missing vertexIndex>=0 check in the groupCode==42 branch allows a heap buffer underflow write when vertexIndex is -1. This matches CVE-2021-21897's heap buffer overflow description and the exact fix applied.

actual_issue_summary: In DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData, when groupCode==42 is processed, vertexIndex (which starts at -1) was not checked for being >= 0, allowing a negative index to be used in the vertices array access (vertices[4*vertexIndex+3]), causing a heap buffer underflow write. The fix adds vertexIndex>=0 checks to both the groupCode<=30 and groupCode==42 branches.

cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

undesired_operation.description: When abbrev->count is 0, the expression 'abbrev->count - 1' underflows to SIZE_MAX (since size_t is unsigned), causing the loop to iterate billions of times and access die->attr_values[i] and abbrev->defs[i] massively out of bounds.
code_snippets: buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i], &die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds heap write leading to crash or arbitrary code execution when processing a crafted DWARF binary with an abbreviation declaration that has count == 0.

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop condition is evaluated: i < abbrev->count - 1. Since abbrev->count is 0 (size_t, unsigned), abbrev->count - 1 underflows to SIZE_MAX.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: 0 < SIZE_MAX is true, so the loop body executes

step_by_step_execution.description: memset writes to die->attr_values[i] which is out of bounds for large i values, and parse_attr_value accesses abbrev->defs[i] also out of bounds

out_state.value: written out of bounds on heap

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches the CVE: when abbrev->count is 0, the unsigned subtraction underflows causing a massive loop that writes out of bounds on the heap. The fix adds an `if (abbrev->count)` guard, which is exactly what the commit does.

actual_issue_summary: When abbrev->count is 0, the loop condition `i < abbrev->count - 1` causes an unsigned integer underflow (0 - 1 = SIZE_MAX for size_t), leading to a heap-based out-of-bounds write via die->attr_values[i] and abbrev->defs[i]. The fix adds a check `if (abbrev->count)` before the loop.

cve_desc: The ip6gre_err function in net/ipv6/ip6_gre.c in the Linux kernel allows remote attackers to have unspecified impact via vectors involving GRE flags in an IPv6 packet, which trigger an out-of-bounds access.

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds read when computing the GRE key for tunnel lookup. The expression `*(((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1)` double-counts `offset` because `p` already points to `skb->data + offset`, but `grehlen` includes `offset` (grehlen = offset + 4 + ...). This causes the read to go `offset` bytes past the validated region.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from skb data. An attacker sending a crafted ICMPv6 error containing a GRE packet with the KEY flag set can read memory beyond the validated packet buffer, potentially causing information disclosure or a kernel crash.

initial_state.value: offset into skb->data where GRE header starts (includes IPv6 header size, ~40 bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: p is set to point to skb->data + offset (the GRE header location)
grehlen is initialized to offset + 4
flags are read from p[0]

out_state.value: attacker-controlled GRE flags with GRE_KEY set

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: GRE_KEY flag is set
Neither GRE_VERSION nor GRE_ROUTING is set
GRE_KEY flag is set

step_by_step_execution.description: grehlen is incremented by 4 for the key field

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: GRE_CSUM is not set in this scenario
pskb_may_pull validates grehlen = offset + 8 bytes from start of skb->data

step_by_step_execution.description: The key is read using *(((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1). Since p = skb->data + offset and grehlen = offset + 8, the access is at p + (grehlen/4 - 1)*4 = (skb->data + offset) + (offset + 8)/4*4 - 4 = skb->data + offset + offset + 4 = skb->data + 2*offset + 4. This is ~offset bytes past the intended location of skb->data + offset + 4.

out_state.value: skb->data + offset + 44 (when offset=40), which is ~40 bytes past the key field at skb->data + offset + 4

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely identifies the CVE-2017-5897 vulnerability: the out-of-bounds read caused by grehlen including offset while p already incorporates offset, leading to double-counting and accessing memory ~40 bytes past the intended location when reading the GRE key.

actual_issue_summary: In ip6gre_err(), grehlen was initialized as `offset + 4` (including the IPv6 header offset), but then used in the expression `*(((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1)` where `p` already points to `skb->data + offset`. This double-counting of offset caused an out-of-bounds read ~40 bytes past the intended GRE key field. The fix changes grehlen to track only the GRE header length and computes the key offset separately.

cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

undesired_operation.description: strcpy copies pipePath (from endpoint.name()) into local.sun_path, a fixed-size buffer of 108 bytes (on Linux) within sockaddr_un, with no length check. An attacker-controlled endpoint name longer than 107 characters overflows the stack buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to arbitrary code execution or crash. An attacker who controls the endpoint name can overwrite the return address or other stack variables.

initial_state.value: uninitialized fixed-size buffer (108 bytes on Linux)

step_by_step_execution.description: pipePath is set from endpoint.name()
strcpy copies pipePath into local.sun_path without length check

judgments.reasoning: CVE-2022-24950 is specifically about a race condition in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId() and acceptNewConnection() due to missing mutex locks, allowing SSH authorization socket hijacking. The finding describes a strcpy buffer overflow which, while fixed in the same commit, is a separate issue and not the vulnerability described by CVE-2022-24950.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2022-24950 is a race condition in UserTerminalRouter where getInfoForId() and acceptNewConnection() access shared state (idInfoMap) without proper locking. The fix adds a recursive_mutex (routerMutex) with lock_guard in both methods. The strcpy→strncpy change in PipeSocketHandler was an unrelated hardening fix in the same commit.

cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

undesired_operation.description: ReadOK reads 104 or 120 bytes into a 64-byte stack buffer 'buffer' when Bitmap_File_Head.biSize is 108 or 124 (BMP Version 4 or 5)
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to arbitrary code execution. An attacker can craft a BMP file with biSize=108 or biSize=124 to overflow the 64-byte stack buffer.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the root cause: when biSize is 108 or 124, ReadOK reads 104 or 120 bytes into a 64-byte stack buffer, causing a buffer overflow. The fix increases the buffer from 64 to 128 bytes, which is exactly what this finding describes. While the CVE description mentions 'heap overflow via ReadImage', the actual commit fix is increasing the stack buffer size, and this finding accurately identifies that vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: Stack buffer overflow in input_bmp_reader: the 64-byte stack buffer 'buffer' is too small for BMP Version 4 (biSize=108) or Version 5 (biSize=124) headers, which require reading 104 or 120 bytes respectively. The fix increases the buffer to 128 bytes.

cve_desc: In the Linux kernel before 5.3.6, there is a use-after-free bug that can be caused by a malicious USB device in the drivers/net/ieee802154/atusb.c driver, aka CID-7fd25e6fc035.

undesired_operation.description: After ieee802154_free_hw(atusb->hw) frees the hw structure (which embeds the atusb private data), the code dereferences atusb->usb_dev in usb_put_dev(atusb->usb_dev), constituting a use-after-free.
undesired_operation.impact: Use-after-free can lead to crash or potentially arbitrary code execution if the freed memory is reallocated with attacker-controlled data between the free and the use.

state.value: points to freed memory (private data embedded in hw, freed by ieee802154_free_hw)

initial_state.value: valid pointer to private data obtained via usb_get_intfdata(interface)
valid pointer to ieee802154_hw structure that embeds atusb as private data

step_by_step_execution.description: ieee802154_unregister_hw(atusb->hw) unregisters the hardware device
ieee802154_free_hw(atusb->hw) frees the hw structure, which also frees the embedded atusb private data

in_state.value: valid pointer to private data embedded in hw

step_by_step_execution.description: usb_put_dev(atusb->usb_dev) dereferences the freed atusb pointer to access usb_dev field

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2019-19525: ieee802154_free_hw frees the hw structure (and the embedded atusb private data), then usb_put_dev(atusb->usb_dev) dereferences the freed atusb. The fix moves usb_put_dev before ieee802154_free_hw.

actual_issue_summary: In atusb_disconnect, ieee802154_free_hw(atusb->hw) frees the hardware descriptor and its embedded private data (atusb), but then usb_put_dev(atusb->usb_dev) dereferences the now-freed atusb pointer. The fix reorders the calls so usb_put_dev runs before ieee802154_free_hw.

cve_desc: TigerVNC version prior to 1.10.1 is vulnerable to stack use-after-return, which occurs due to incorrect usage of stack memory in ZRLEDecoder. If decoding routine would throw an exception, ZRLEDecoder may try to access stack variable, which has been already freed during the process of stack unwinding. Exploitation of this vulnerability could potentially result into remote code execution. This attack appear to be exploitable via network connectivity.

undesired_operation.description: In the plain RLE decode path, the variable 'len' (signed int) accumulates attacker-controlled byte values via 'len += b' in a do-while loop. An attacker sending a crafted ZRLE stream with a long sequence of 0xFF bytes causes signed integer overflow, which is undefined behavior (UB) per the C/C++ standard. Once UB occurs, the subsequent bounds check 'end - ptr < len' is not guaranteed to behave correctly. On two's-complement systems, the overflowed len becomes negative, causing the check 'end - ptr < len' (positive < negative) to evaluate false, bypassing the exception. While the write loop may not immediately execute with a negative len on such systems, the same integer overflow UB also exists in the palette RLE path, and the undefined behavior means the program's execution is unconstrained after the overflow point — the bounds check cannot be relied upon to prevent a stack buffer overflow of buf[64*64].
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service or potential code execution. The signed integer overflow is undefined behavior, rendering the subsequent bounds check unreliable. On two's-complement hardware the bounds check is bypassed (len becomes negative). Depending on compiler optimizations around the UB, this could lead to stack buffer overflow of buf[64*64] and arbitrary code execution. This is CVE-2019-15694.

state.value: overflowed signed int after accumulating attacker-controlled 0xFF bytes

initial_state.value: points to buf (stack array of 64*64 PIXEL_T)

step_by_step_execution.description: Enter plain RLE path (palSize==0, rle==true), initialize ptr and end
Read pixel and initialize len=1
Accumulate len via do-while loop reading 0xFF bytes repeatedly

out_state.value: overflowed (e.g. negative on two's complement after ~8.4 million iterations for 32-bit int)
last byte read, 255 until final non-255

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: With len overflowed to a negative value, (end - ptr) which is positive is not less than a negative len, so the bounds check passes

step_by_step_execution.description: Write loop 'while (len-- > 0) *ptr++ = pix' — with negative len the loop does not execute on two's complement, but the UB from overflow means compiler behavior is unconstrained

judgments.reasoning: CVE-2019-15691 is about stack use-after-return in ZlibInStream, where after an exception during decoding, the ZlibInStream destructor accesses stack memory (the underlying stream) that is no longer valid. The fix changes removeUnderlying() to flushUnderlying() + setUnderlying(NULL,0) and moves the NULL check to decompress(). The finding describes a completely different issue — a signed integer overflow in the RLE length accumulation — which the finding itself identifies as CVE-2019-15694, not CVE-2019-15691. The code in the diff is unchanged between vulnerable and fixed versions for the RLE path; the only change is the cleanup at the end of the function.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a stack use-after-return in ZlibInStream. When removeUnderlying() is called (or during destruction), the ZlibInStream may access the underlying stream pointer which points to stack memory that has been freed during stack unwinding after an exception. The fix replaces removeUnderlying() with flushUnderlying() + setUnderlying(NULL, 0), and moves the NULL underlying check into decompress() to prevent accessing invalid memory.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming STUN message contains an ERROR-CODE attribute, the header length is not checked before performing a subtraction operation, potentially resulting in an integer underflow scenario. This issue affects all users that use STUN. A malicious actor located within the victim’s network may forge and send a specially crafted UDP (STUN) message that could remotely execute arbitrary code on the victim’s machine. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

undesired_operation.description: When attr->hdr.length (read from attacker-controlled buf) is less than 4, the expression 'attr->hdr.length - 4' produces a negative value assigned to value.slen. pj_strdup then interprets this negative slen as a huge size_t when calling memcpy/allocation, causing a heap buffer over-read or crash.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash, or information disclosure via heap over-read. An attacker sends a crafted STUN message with an ERROR-CODE attribute whose length field is less than 4.

initial_state.value: unset, will be read from buf via GETATTRHDR

step_by_step_execution.description: GETATTRHDR reads the attribute header from buf, including attr->hdr.length which is attacker-controlled
value.slen is computed as attr->hdr.length - 4
pj_strdup is called with value.slen = -2; when cast to size_t for allocation/memcpy this becomes a very large positive number

out_state.value: -2 interpreted as huge size_t in memcpy

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the CVE-2021-37706 vulnerability: the missing check on attr->hdr.length before the subtraction of 4, leading to integer underflow. The fix adds exactly the check for value.slen < 0. The CWE-191 matches the CVE's CWE.

actual_issue_summary: In decode_errcode_attr, when a STUN ERROR-CODE attribute has a header length less than 4, the subtraction `attr->hdr.length - 4` produces a negative value for value.slen. This negative value is then used in pj_strdup, where it gets interpreted as a very large unsigned size, leading to a heap buffer over-read or potential remote code execution. The fix clamps value.slen to 0 if it becomes negative.

cve_desc: ImageMagick before 7.0.8-50 has a memory leak vulnerability in the function ReadBMPImage in coders/bmp.c.

undesired_operation.description: In the 1-bit BMP pixel decoding case, colormap index values are set without validation against the colormap size. When bmp_info.number_colors=1, the colormap has only 1 entry (index 0), but the decoder can produce index=1 from pixel data bits. Unlike the 4-bit and 8-bit cases which call IsValidColormapIndex, the 1-bit case directly calls SetPixelIndex. When SyncImage is subsequently called, it reads image->colormap[1] which is out of bounds.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds heap read: adjacent heap memory is interpreted as a colormap entry (PixelPacket), leaking heap data into the output image pixels. Could result in information disclosure.

step_by_step_execution.description: image->colors is set from bmp_info.number_colors
index is computed from pixel data bit
SetPixelIndex stores index=1
SyncImage reads colormap[1] which is out of bounds

judgments.reasoning: The CVE-2019-13133 is about a memory leak (CWE-401) in ReadBMPImage. The actual fix changes the error handling path when GetNextImageInList returns NULL during multi-image BMP processing: instead of calling DestroyImageList and returning immediately (skipping CloseBlob), the fix sets status=MagickFalse, breaks out of the loop, and goes through normal cleanup including CloseBlob. The finding describes a completely different issue - an out-of-bounds colormap read in the 1-bit BMP case - which is unrelated to the memory leak vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: Memory leak when AcquireNextImage succeeds but GetNextImageInList returns NULL during multi-image BMP processing. The old code called DestroyImageList and returned immediately, skipping CloseBlob. The fix breaks out of the loop, calls CloseBlob, then conditionally destroys the image list, ensuring proper resource cleanup.

cve_desc: st21nfca_connectivity_event_received in drivers/nfc/st21nfca/se.c in the Linux kernel through 5.16.12 has EVT_TRANSACTION buffer overflows because of untrusted length parameters.

undesired_operation.description: memcpy copies transaction->aid_len bytes (attacker-controlled via skb->data[1], up to 255) into the transaction buffer which was allocated as skb->len - 2 bytes, causing a heap buffer overflow
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow allowing kernel memory corruption, potentially leading to privilege escalation or denial of service

step_by_step_execution.description: Event is ST21NFCA_EVT_TRANSACTION, entering that case
transaction buffer allocated with size skb->len - 2

out_state.value: heap buffer of skb->len - 2 bytes, e.g. 8 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: transaction->aid_len read directly from skb->data[1] without validation
memcpy copies aid_len bytes into transaction->aid, overflowing the allocated buffer

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the first buffer overflow fixed by the patch: the missing bounds check on transaction->aid_len before memcpy into transaction->aid.

undesired_operation.description: memcpy copies transaction->params_len bytes (attacker-controlled via skb->data[aid_len+3], up to 255) into transaction->params, which can overflow the heap buffer allocated as skb->len - 2 bytes
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow allowing kernel memory corruption, potentially leading to privilege escalation or denial of service

step_by_step_execution.description: transaction->params_len read from skb->data[aid_len+3] without bounds check
memcpy copies params_len bytes into transaction->params, overflowing the heap allocation

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the second buffer overflow fixed by the patch: the missing bounds check on transaction->params_len before memcpy into transaction->params.

actual_issue_summary: In st21nfca_connectivity_event_received, the EVT_TRANSACTION handler reads aid_len and params_len directly from untrusted skb->data without validating them against buffer sizes. This allows heap buffer overflows via memcpy into transaction->aid and transaction->params. The fix adds bounds checks for both length fields before the respective memcpy calls.

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0102.

undesired_operation.description: Signed integer overflow in the 'extra' variable (type int) during the first pass causes the buffer allocation size 'len' to be much smaller than the actual bytes written in the second pass, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow allowing potential arbitrary code execution. An attacker who can control the input string (e.g., via a crafted Vim script) can overwrite heap memory.

judgments.reasoning: The finding incorrectly identifies the root cause as signed integer overflow in 'extra'. The actual vulnerability is that the first scanning loop doesn't properly skip over characters inside \<...> key forms. When interpolation is enabled and a \<...> sequence contains '{', the first loop mistakenly treats '{' as the start of a string interpolation expression, terminating early. This makes (p - *arg) smaller than it should be, resulting in an undersized buffer allocation. The second pass then writes past the buffer. The fix adds find_special_key() to properly skip the \<...> form in the first loop. The finding correctly identifies the consequence (heap buffer overflow from undersized allocation) but completely misattributes the mechanism to integer overflow rather than incorrect parsing/skipping of special key sequences.

actual_issue_summary: In eval_string(), when processing string interpolation, the first scanning loop did not properly skip over \<Key> forms (e.g., \<C-{>). If a '{' appeared inside a \<...> sequence, the loop would mistake it for the start of an interpolation expression and stop early, causing the buffer size calculation (p - *arg + extra) to be too small. The second pass would then write beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap-based buffer overflow. The fix adds find_special_key() in the first loop to properly skip past the entire \<...> sequence.

cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in jetp3852_print_page() in devices/gdev3852.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to escalate privileges via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

undesired_operation.description: memset(end_data, 0, 7) writes 7 bytes starting at end_data, which can be as high as data + DATA_SIZE (when line_size == DATA_SIZE and the last byte is non-zero). This writes 7 bytes past the end of the stack buffer 'data'.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow of up to 7 bytes, potentially overwriting adjacent stack variables (plane_data or saved frame pointer/return address), leading to crash or arbitrary code execution.

state.value: data + DATA_SIZE (when line_size == DATA_SIZE and last byte is non-zero)

initial_state.value: uninitialized stack buffer of DATA_SIZE bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: line_size is computed from device properties via gdev_mem_bytes_per_scan_line

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: If line_size > DATA_SIZE the function returns an error. So line_size <= DATA_SIZE.

step_by_step_execution.description: end_data is set to data + line_size
Trailing zeros are stripped from end_data backwards, but if line_size == DATA_SIZE and the last byte is non-zero, end_data stays at data + DATA_SIZE

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The line has non-zero data so end_data > data

step_by_step_execution.description: memset(end_data, 0, 7) writes 7 zero bytes starting at data + DATA_SIZE, which is past the end of the data[] buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the buffer overflow caused by memset(end_data, 0, 7) when end_data points to the end of the data[] buffer. This is exactly the code removed by the fix, and the CVE description confirms a buffer overflow in jetp3852_print_page(). The fix replaced this localized padding with a full buffer initialization at declaration time.

actual_issue_summary: The data[] stack buffer was not initialized, and memset(end_data, 0, 7) could write up to 7 bytes past the end of data[] when line_size equals DATA_SIZE. Additionally, uninitialized bytes in data[] beyond line_size could contain values > 7, causing out-of-bounds reads on the spr40/spr8/spr2 lookup tables. The fix initializes the entire data[] buffer to zero at the start and removes the dangerous memset(end_data, 0, 7) call.

cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

undesired_operation.description: In the match copy path where match_offset > window_posn (window wrap case), match_length bytes are copied via rundest starting at &window[window_posn] with no check that window_posn + match_length <= window_size. This allows writing past the end of the window buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — attacker-controlled match_length causes writes past the allocated window buffer, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution

initial_state.value: restored from qtm->window_posn, some value < window_size
unset, determined by selector path from crafted input
fixed size of allocated window buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: Selector 6 is decoded from the compressed stream, leading to variable length match path

out_state.value: attacker-controlled value derived from qtm->length_base[sym] + extra + 5, can be large

step_by_step_execution.description: match_offset is decoded from the compressed stream

step_by_step_execution.branch_taken: true (no check exists in vulnerable version)
step_by_step_execution.reasoning: No bounds check exists before the copy loop, so when match_length is large enough that window_posn + match_length exceeds window_size, execution proceeds to the copy

step_by_step_execution.description: rundest is set to &window[window_posn] and i = match_length bytes are copied, writing past the end of the window buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly identifies the missing bounds check that the fix adds: window_posn + match_length > qtm->window_size. This is precisely CVE-2010-1311 — a crafted CAB archive with Quantum compression can cause memory corruption by having match_length extend past the window buffer.

actual_issue_summary: The qtm_decompress function lacked a check that window_posn + match_length does not exceed window_size before copying match data. A crafted CAB archive using Quantum compression could supply a match_length that causes writes beyond the allocated window buffer, resulting in memory corruption and crash. The fix adds an explicit bounds check returning CL_EFORMAT if the match would run over the window boundary.

cve_desc: libmobi is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Write

undesired_operation.description: When offset is negative, the function only checks that the source pointer doesn't go before the buffer start (buf->offset >= aoffset), but does NOT check that buf->offset + len <= buf->maxlen. This allows memmove to write past the end of the buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow write — attacker can corrupt memory past the buffer boundary, potentially achieving code execution or denial of service

initial_state.value: attacker-influenced, large enough that buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen

step_by_step_execution.description: aoffset is computed as abs(offset)
source is set to buf->data + buf->offset

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: offset is negative, so we enter the else branch
buf->offset >= aoffset, so the bounds check passes and we don't return early. However, buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen is not checked.

relevant_state.value: large, such that buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen

step_by_step_execution.description: source -= aoffset
memmove writes len bytes from source to buf->data + buf->offset, writing past the end of the buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches the CVE fix: the missing check (buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen) in the negative offset branch allows an out-of-bounds write via memmove, which is precisely what the patch adds.

actual_issue_summary: In mobi_buffer_move, when offset is negative, the function fails to check that buf->offset + len doesn't exceed buf->maxlen, allowing memmove to write beyond the allocated buffer. The fix adds this missing bounds check.

cve_desc: gio/gsocketclient.c in GNOME GLib 2.59.2 does not ensure that a parent GTask remains alive during the execution of a connection-attempting enumeration, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (g_socket_client_connected_callback mishandling and application crash) via a crafted web site, as demonstrated by GNOME Web (aka Epiphany).

undesired_operation.description: When data is NULL (task already completed, as noted by the comment), and g_socket_connection_connect_finish succeeds, data is dereferenced without a NULL check at 'data->socket = g_steal_pointer(&attempt->socket)', causing a NULL pointer dereference.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (NULL pointer dereference). An attacker who can influence the timing of connection completions could trigger this race condition where the task completes and sets data to NULL before this callback fires for a successful connection.

state.value: NULL (set to NULL by another completed attempt via attempt_entry->data = NULL)

initial_state.value: attempt->data, which may be NULL if another attempt already completed and set attempt_entry->data = NULL

step_by_step_execution.description: data is read from attempt->data

in_state.value: NULL (set by successful completion of another attempt)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: data is NULL, so the short-circuit evaluation makes the whole condition false, skipping the early return
The connection succeeds, so the function does not return an error, and execution continues past the error-handling block

step_by_step_execution.description: data->socket is dereferenced to store the stolen pointer, but data is NULL

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the core of CVE-2019-9633: when the parent GTask completes and sets data to NULL on other connection attempts, subsequent callbacks can dereference NULL data. The fix removes the `attempt_entry->data = NULL` pattern and instead uses proper cancellation checks to prevent accessing stale data.

undesired_operation.description: When data is NULL (task already completed) and g_socket_connection_connect_finish fails, and the attempt's cancellable is not cancelled, data->connectable is dereferenced in clarify_connect_error without a NULL check, causing a NULL pointer dereference.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash. An attacker who can cause a connection failure after the task has already completed triggers a NULL pointer dereference.

state.value: NULL (set to NULL by another completed attempt via attempt_entry->data = NULL)

initial_state.value: attempt->data, which may be NULL if task already completed

step_by_step_execution.description: data is read from attempt->data

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: data is NULL so condition short-circuits to false
Connection fails, entering the error handling block
The attempt's cancellable has not been cancelled

step_by_step_execution.description: data->connectable is dereferenced in clarify_connect_error, but data is NULL

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes another path to the same CVE-2019-9633 vulnerability. When data is NULL (because another attempt completed and set data to NULL), and the connection fails without the attempt being cancelled, the code dereferences NULL data. The fix restructures the logic to properly check cancellation state and avoid this path.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2019-9633: When one connection attempt succeeds, other attempts have their `data` field set to NULL (`attempt_entry->data = NULL`). When callbacks for those other attempts later fire, they can dereference the NULL `data` pointer, causing crashes. The fix removes the `data = NULL` pattern and instead uses proper cancellation tracking (via `g_cancellable_is_cancelled` and `task_completed_or_cancelled`) to ensure the parent GTask remains alive and callbacks handle completion/cancellation correctly.

cve_desc: Integer Overflow or Wraparound in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

undesired_operation.description: In the KS_VER_SCROLLBAR handling loop, after the first iteration completes and j is incremented past the first event's data, the loop condition reads tp[j], tp[j+1], and tp[j+2] without checking that j+2 < len. This causes an out-of-bounds read past the valid input data.
code_snippets:
for (i = 0; tp[j] == CSI && tp[j + 1] == KS_VER_SCROLLBAR
						     && tp[j + 2] != NUL; ++i)
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read that could leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash if it crosses a page boundary. In GUI mode, an attacker who can inject events into the typeahead buffer could trigger this.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE fix adds `tp[len] = NUL` to NUL-terminate the typeahead buffer, preventing various string operations (getdigits, atoi, string scanning) from reading past the end. While the NUL termination would incidentally stop the scrollbar loop (since NUL != CSI), the scrollbar code is GUI-only and not the primary vulnerability. The CVE's test case involves command-line completion operations, not scrollbar events. The finding describes a different code path than what the CVE actually addresses.

undesired_operation.description: In the KS_HOR_SCROLLBAR handling loop, after the first iteration completes and j is incremented past the first event's data, the loop condition reads tp[j], tp[j+1], and tp[j+2] without checking that j+2 < len. This causes an out-of-bounds read past the valid input data.
code_snippets:
for (i = 0; tp[j] == CSI && tp[j + 1] == KS_HOR_SCROLLBAR
						     && tp[j + 2] != NUL; ++i)
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read that could leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash if it crosses a page boundary.

judgments.reasoning: Same reasoning as Finding 0. The actual CVE is about the typeahead buffer not being NUL-terminated, causing out-of-bounds reads in string processing functions like getdigits() and atoi(). The fix adds `tp[len] = NUL`. While this NUL termination would also stop the horizontal scrollbar loop from over-reading, this GUI-only scrollbar code path is not what the CVE describes. The CVE's test case and exploitation path involve command-line operations, not scrollbar events.

actual_issue_summary: The typeahead buffer in check_termcode() was not NUL-terminated after the valid data region (tp[0..len-1]). This allowed various string-processing operations within the function (such as getdigits(), atoi(), and other string scanning loops) to read past the end of valid typeahead data, causing an out-of-bounds read/write. The fix adds `tp[len] = NUL` to place a NUL terminator immediately after the valid data, preventing any string operations from going past the end of the typeahead buffer.

cve_desc: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 does not check a certain return value from the read_mapping_page function before calling kmap, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (system crash) via a crafted hfsplus filesystem image.

undesired_operation.description: read_mapping_page() returns an ERR_PTR on failure, which is passed unchecked to kmap(), causing a kernel page fault when the resulting garbage pointer is dereferenced.
code_snippets:
page = read_mapping_page(mapping, offset / PAGE_CACHE_BITS, NULL);
curr = pptr = kmap(page);
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via kernel crash (NULL or invalid pointer dereference). A crafted HFS+ filesystem image with a volume header claiming more total allocation blocks than the allocation bitmap file actually contains will trigger this when mounting and allocating blocks.

step_by_step_execution.description: read_mapping_page() is called and returns an ERR_PTR value (e.g., -EIO encoded as a pointer) due to a corrupted HFS+ filesystem image

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The vulnerable code has no IS_ERR check, so execution proceeds unconditionally to kmap(page)

step_by_step_execution.description: kmap(page) is called with the error pointer, producing a garbage kernel virtual address; this is then assigned to pptr and curr
Dereferencing *curr triggers a kernel page fault / oops, crashing the system

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2008-4934: the unchecked return value of read_mapping_page() is passed to kmap(), causing a kernel crash on a crafted HFS+ image. The fix adds IS_ERR() checks at all three call sites.

actual_issue_summary: hfsplus_block_allocate() in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c does not check whether read_mapping_page() returns an error (ERR_PTR) before passing the result to kmap(). On a crafted HFS+ filesystem image, read_mapping_page() can fail and return an error pointer, which when passed to kmap() and subsequently dereferenced causes a kernel oops/crash (denial of service). The fix adds IS_ERR() checks after all three read_mapping_page() calls in the function.

cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in iOctetsRemain accumulation (iOctetsRemain * 10 + c - '0') can cause it to wrap to a negative value. This negative value is then used as the 'octatesToCopy' length parameter in memcpy, which gets implicitly cast to a very large size_t, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Remote code execution or crash — attacker sends a crafted TCP message with a very large octet count that overflows to a negative integer, leading to a massive heap buffer overflow via memcpy.

state.value: overflowed negative value due to unbounded multiplication by 10

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled digit characters from TCP stream

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker sends many digit characters in the octet count field of a TCP framed message, repeatedly entering eInOctetCnt state

out_state.value: growing large value from repeated *10 + digit

step_by_step_execution.description: iOctetsRemain is multiplied by 10 and added to without any bounds check, eventually overflowing the int type

out_state.value: negative value due to signed integer overflow

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: After accumulating enough digits, the SP terminator is received, transitioning to eInMsg state with the overflowed iOctetsRemain

step_by_step_execution.description: In octet counting framing mode, octatesToCopy is assigned from the overflowed iOctetsRemain
memcpy is called with octatesToCopy implicitly cast to size_t, becoming a very large unsigned value, causing heap buffer overflow or crash

in_state.value: negative int, cast to huge size_t

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the integer overflow in iOctetsRemain (CWE-190) that is the root cause of CVE-2018-16881. The fix adds a bounds check (iOctetsRemain <= 200000000) to prevent the overflow, which directly matches this finding.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2018-16881 is an integer overflow in rsyslog's imptcp module where the octet count (iOctetsRemain) is accumulated without bounds checking via `iOctetsRemain * 10 + c - '0'`. An attacker can send a specially crafted message with an extremely large octet count that overflows the integer, leading to a crash (denial of service). The fix adds a cap of 200000000 on iOctetsRemain before multiplication.

cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

undesired_operation.description: When dl->length is smaller than so_ext_len, the expression 'dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len' underflows because all values are size_t (unsigned). This produces a pointer far outside the dl->str buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read in my_strcasecmp.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read that could crash the server (denial of service) or leak sensitive memory contents. An attacker can trigger this by providing a very short (e.g., empty) plugin library name.

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled, can be 0 or any small value
pointer to plugin library name string

step_by_step_execution.description: so_ext_len is computed as sizeof(SO_EXT) - 1
The expression dl->length - so_ext_len is computed. Since both are size_t (unsigned), when dl->length < so_ext_len this wraps to a very large value.

out_state.value: pointer far before dl->str buffer (wraps to huge offset)

step_by_step_execution.description: my_strcasecmp is called with the bogus pointer, reading memory out of bounds

in_state.value: wild pointer due to unsigned underflow

out_state.value: out-of-bounds read from invalid memory location

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact vulnerability fixed by the patch: when dl->length < so_ext_len, the unsigned subtraction wraps around, producing an out-of-bounds pointer passed to my_strcasecmp. The fix adds exactly the check 'dl->length < so_ext_len' before the subtraction. While the CVE describes it as use-after-free/use-after-poison, the underlying root cause is the missing length check that the finding accurately describes. The CWE differs (CWE-125 vs CWE-416) but this is a matter of classification; the actual bug is the same.

actual_issue_summary: When dl->length is smaller than so_ext_len, the unsigned arithmetic in 'dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len' wraps around, causing my_strcasecmp to read from an invalid memory location. The fix adds a check 'dl->length < so_ext_len' before performing the subtraction, short-circuiting to the branch that appends the extension instead.

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.2.

undesired_operation.description: R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT reads 2 bytes from buffer at offset 6 (to get number_of_classes) without verifying that sz >= 8, causing an out-of-bounds read on a small buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure or crash — attacker provides a crafted Java class file with a small inner_classes attribute, causing a read past the buffer boundary.

judgments.reasoning: The finding identifies the missing bounds check before reading number_of_classes at offset 6. The fix adds exactly this check: `if (buf_offset + offset + 8 > sz) return NULL;` before that read. While the CVE is categorized as CWE-787 (heap-based buffer overflow), the underlying issue is insufficient bounds checking on buffer access, and this finding correctly identifies one of the two missing/insufficient checks that the fix addresses. The OOB read at offset 6 is a direct component of the vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: Missing and insufficient bounds checks in r_bin_java_inner_classes_attr_new: (1) No bounds check before reading number_of_classes at offset 6, and (2) the loop bounds check used `offset + 8 > sz` instead of `buf_offset + offset + 8 > sz`, allowing out-of-bounds buffer access when parsing crafted Java class files.

cve_desc: LibVNC before commit 7b1ef0ffc4815cab9a96c7278394152bdc89dc4d contains heap out-of-bound write vulnerability inside structure in VNC client code that can result remote code execution

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in `hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8))` allows the size check against RFB_BUFFER_SIZE to be bypassed. A small number of bytes is read into client->buffer, but the loop iterates hdr.nSubrects times (a very large number), reading far past the end of client->buffer.
code_snippets:
if (hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8)) > RFB_BUFFER_SIZE || !ReadFromRFBServer(client, client->buffer, hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8))))
	return FALSE;
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from client->buffer. An attacker-controlled malicious RFB server can cause information disclosure of heap/stack contents or crash the client (denial of service). The out-of-bounds data is passed to GotFillRect which may render it, leaking memory contents.

out_state.value: attacker-controlled uint32_t, e.g. a value near UINT32_MAX/(4+(BPP/8))+1 causing overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: The multiplication hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8)) is computed for the size check. Due to integer overflow, the product wraps around to a small value.

out_state.value: small value after overflow, e.g. <= RFB_BUFFER_SIZE
